EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early onset to the Southwest Monsoon brought above normal precipitation to the area, especially mountain locations where some areas received over 2 inches more precipitation than normal. Exceptional(D4) drought conditions were removed from all areas of southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The month started with Exceptional(D4) drought across the north with Moderate(D1) drought over the southeast.  Conditions across the north improved to Extreme(D3) drought over the north with continued Moderate(D1) drought at the end of June. The Deming Airport reported 1.35 inches of precipitation which was 0.80 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early onset to the Southwest Monsoon brought above normal precipitation to the area, especially mountain locations where some areas received over 2 inches more precipitation than normal. Exceptional(D4) drought conditions were removed from all areas of southern New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early onset to the Southwest Monsoon brought above normal precipitation to the area, especially mountain locations where some areas received over 2 inches more precipitation than normal. Exceptional(D4) drought conditions were removed from all areas of southern New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent upper-level storm system moving slowly southeast toward NM on October 23, 2022 combined with a deep area of surface low pressure along the Front Range to produce widespread strong winds across the region. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph first developed along the central high terrain during the early morning hours before spreading into the nearby highlands and northeast plains of NM through the afternoon. The highest wind gusts impacted the area around Ruidoso where gusts near 70 mph were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gallup airport reported a peak wind gust to 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another month of above normal precipitation brought almost all of southern New Mexico out of Severe(D2) drought or worse.  By the end of October, only a a small area around the New Mexico Bootheel remained in Severe drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe(D2) drought remained over the far northwest part of the zone to start the month and improved to no drought over the southeast.  By the end of October, all areas were in Abnormally Dry(D0) drought except the southeast where no drought existed. The Cooperative Observer at New Mexico State University reported 1.56 inches of precipitation which was 0.74 inches above normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A social media post on Facebook showed a well-defined funnel cloud over the west mesa near Belen, NM on October 8, 2022. Time and location are estimated from the Facebook post.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper-level storm system moving east through southern Arizona into New Mexico interacted with a strong backdoor cold front sliding into eastern NM on October 15-16, 2022. Strong east winds developed below canyons in the Rio Grande Valley on the morning of the 16th with gusts of 45 to 60 mph from near Placitas south to Albuquerque, Abo Pass, and Carrizozo. The strongest winds impacted the Albuquerque Sunport where a gust up to 60 mph occurred. Widespread showers and thunderstorms with areas of rain, fog, and very high terrain snow also occurred with this storm system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust up to 60 mph was reported at the Albuquerque Sunport. Gap winds below Tijeras Canyon also reached up to 65 mph at a couple isolated locations around Embudo and Four Hills. KOAT reported a downed power line in the Nob Hill area of Berkeley and Wellesley. Damage amount is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very active end of December 2021, the overall storm track wasn't favorable to New Mexico in January 2022. High winds were once again the most dominant feature across northern and central New Mexico during the first half of the month. A couple winter storm systems finally brought rounds of snow mainly to central and eastern New Mexico during the latter half of the month, but poor liquid equivalent totals did nothing to improve drought conditions across the area. In fact, January 2022 saw the return of exceptional drought conditions across portions of Union and Colfax counties. Drought conditions across the remainder of the state worsened with about 78 percent in at least severe drought conditions. About 30 percent of that was categorized as extreme or exceptional drought. Precipitation totals across much of northern and central New Mexico generally ranged from one quarter to one half inch. High terrain areas across north central and western New Mexico fared better with precipitation totals closer to one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from December 2021 continued in January 2022.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As this event began in December 2021, this episode is a continuation and repeated here. A pair of storm systems helped to usher in a new year as they impacted much of northern and central New Mexico on December 31, 2021 and January 1, 2022. The first storm system was a closed upper-level low pressure system off the coast of Baja California. This system moved northeastward through the area on December 31, 2021, bringing a deep tap of subtropical moisture with it. This system brought the first round of heavy snowfall to the mountainous terrain areas. The second, much colder storm system then tracked southeastward across the state on New Years' Day. Another round of heavy snow fell across much of northern and western New Mexico. With an associated backdoor cold front moving into eastern New Mexico early on January 1, 2022, this cold air advection also set the stage for additional snow accumulations across the eastern plains. Much of northern and central New Mexico received measurable snowfall with the main exceptions being the middle and lower Rio Grande Valley, including Albuquerque. The highest snowfall totals were received in the northern mountains where 10 to 20 inches of snow fell over the two day period. Due to snow-packed and icy roads, difficult to severe driving conditions were reported along several major roadways, including Interstate 40 near Gallup, Moriarty, and Tucumcari. Key Impacts: poor travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling across the area on December 31, 2021, and continued through the overnight hours. A member of the public near Angel Fire measured seventeen inches of new snow on January 1, 2022.

The first storm system was a closed upper-level low pressure system off the coast of Baja California. This system moved northeastward through the area on December 31, 2021, bringing a deep tap of subtropical moisture with it. This system brought the first round of heavy snowfall to the mountainous terrain areas. The second, much colder storm system then tracked southeastward across the state on New Years' Day. Another round of heavy snow fell across much of northern and western New Mexico. With an associated backdoor cold front moving into eastern New Mexico early on January 1, 2022, this cold air advection also set the stage for additional snow accumulations across the eastern plains. Much of northern and central New Mexico received measurable snowfall with the main exceptions being the middle and lower Rio Grande Valley, including Albuquerque. The highest snowfall totals were received in the northern mountains where 10 to 20 inches of snow fell over the two day period. Due to snow-packed and icy roads, difficult to severe driving conditions were reported along several major roadways, including Interstate 40 near Gallup, Moriarty, and Tucumcari. Key Impacts: poor travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling across the area early on December 31, 2021, and continued through the overnight hours. By January 1, 2022, a SNOTEL site near Bonito Lake received an estimated ten inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very active end of December 2021, the overall storm track wasn't favorable to New Mexico in January 2022. High winds were once again the most dominant feature across northern and central New Mexico during the first half of the month. A couple winter storm systems finally brought rounds of snow mainly to central and eastern New Mexico during the latter half of the month, but poor liquid equivalent totals did nothing to improve drought conditions across the area. In fact, January 2022 saw the return of exceptional drought conditions across portions of Union and Colfax counties. Drought conditions across the remainder of the state worsened with about 78 percent in at least severe drought conditions. About 30 percent of that was categorized as extreme or exceptional drought. Precipitation totals across much of northern and central New Mexico generally ranged from one quarter to one half inch. High terrain areas across north central and western New Mexico fared better with precipitation totals closer to one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from December 2021 continued in January 2022. A small portion of the area did improve to moderate drought conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level storm system became organized over the Great Basin area on January 25, 2022. Meanwhile, a backdoor cold front moved westward across eastern New Mexico ahead of this system. As this winter storm tracked eastward across New Mexico through January 26th, it brought a round of snow to central and eastern portions of the state. The heaviest snow totals were measured along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and adjacent highlands due to the upslope flow from the backdoor cold front. These totals were generally between 5 and 7 inches. Snow totals across other portions of the northern mountains and eastern plains were lighter and generally less than 6 inches. Snow packed and icy roadways which resulted in difficult driving conditions were common across much of the area where snow fell. Unfortunately, one indirect fatality was attributed to this system. The fatality and one injury occurred during a two-vehicle collision on icy roadways near Santa Fe. Impacts: difficult driving conditions, vehicle accidents, indirect fatality, indirect injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter south-southeast of San Augustin measured seven inches of new snow. Snow packed and icy roads resulted in difficult driving conditions along roadways, including Interstate 25 in the Las Vegas area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Increasing westerly flow across eastern New Mexico resulted in breezy to windy conditions across the area on January 8, 2022. With these downsloping winds, the usual windy areas across east central New Mexico measured wind gusts near 40 to 50 mph. The exception was Clines Corners which measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph briefly during the late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station in Clines Corners measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station in Clines Corners measured a peak sustained wind speed of 43 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a storm system raced southeastward from the Pacific Northwest through the central Rockies on January 14, 2022, it sent a strong backdoor cold front through the eastern plains of New Mexico. With a strong pressure gradient along this boundary combined with strong upper-level winds, surface winds increased along this front as it tracked south and west through New Mexico. Wind gusts across eastern New Mexico were generally about 45 to 55 mph with the strongest winds felt across the northeast corner of the state where a 60 mph wind gust was measured in Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station in Clayton measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft over New Mexico increased in response to a strong upper-level jet diving into the central Rockies on January 4, 2022. As 700mb wind strengthened over the peaks of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and the Sandia/Manzano Mountains, windy conditions developed across the high terrain and adjacent highlands. Wind gusts up to 58 mph were reported near Sandia Peak as well as along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station at Sandia Crest measured a peak wind speed of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Northwest flow aloft persisted over New Mexico on January 5, 2022, and as another upper-level jet moved into the central Rockies, winds increased again for the central high terrain areas. The strongest 700mb were confined to the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and adjacent highlands. While breezy to windy conditions were common in this area, high wind speeds were localized and only measured briefly in Las Vegas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Las Vegas measured a peak wind gust of 63 mph.

As this winter storm tracked eastward across New Mexico through January 26th, it brought a round of snow to central and eastern portions of the state. The heaviest snow totals were measured along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and adjacent highlands due to the upslope flow from the backdoor cold front. These totals were generally between 5 and 7 inches. Snow totals across other portions of the northern mountains and eastern plains were lighter and generally less than 6 inches. Snow packed and icy roadways which resulted in difficult driving conditions were common across much of the area where snow fell. Unfortunately, one indirect fatality was attributed to this system. The fatality and one injury occurred during a two-vehicle collision on icy roadways near Santa Fe. Impacts: difficult driving conditions, vehicle accidents, indirect fatality, indirect injury.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter east-southeast of Maes measured seven inches of new snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After a very active end of December 2021, the overall storm track wasn't favorable to New Mexico in January 2022. High winds were once again the most dominant feature across northern and central New Mexico during the first half of the month. A couple winter storm systems finally brought rounds of snow mainly to central and eastern New Mexico during the latter half of the month, but poor liquid equivalent totals did nothing to improve drought conditions across the area. In fact, January 2022 saw the return of exceptional drought conditions across portions of Union and Colfax counties. Drought conditions across the remainder of the state worsened with about 78 percent in at least severe drought conditions. About 30 percent of that was categorized as extreme or exceptional drought. Precipitation totals across much of northern and central New Mexico generally ranged from one quarter to one half inch. High terrain areas across north central and western New Mexico fared better with precipitation totals closer to one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from December 2021 continued in January 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early onset to the Southwest Monsoon brought above normal precipitation to the area, especially mountain locations where some areas received over 2 inches more precipitation than normal. Exceptional(D4) drought conditions were removed from all areas of southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in Severe(D2) drought through the entire month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As this event began in December 2021, this episode is a continuation and repeated here. A pair of storm systems helped to usher in a new year as they impacted much of northern and central New Mexico on December 31, 2021 and January 1, 2022. The first storm system was a closed upper-level low pressure system off the coast of Baja California. This system moved northeastward through the area on December 31, 2021, bringing a deep tap of subtropical moisture with it. This system brought the first round of heavy snowfall to the mountainous terrain areas. The second, much colder storm system then tracked southeastward across the state on New Years' Day. Another round of heavy snow fell across much of northern and western New Mexico. With an associated backdoor cold front moving into eastern New Mexico early on January 1, 2022, this cold air advection also set the stage for additional snow accumulations across the eastern plains. Much of northern and central New Mexico received measurable snowfall with the main exceptions being the middle and lower Rio Grande Valley, including Albuquerque. The highest snowfall totals were received in the northern mountains where 10 to 20 inches of snow fell over the two day period. Due to snow-packed and icy roads, difficult to severe driving conditions were reported along several major roadways, including Interstate 40 near Gallup, Moriarty, and Tucumcari. Key Impacts: poor travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow began falling across the area on December 31, 2021, and continued through the overnight hours. A trained spotter near Regina measured twelve inches of new snow on January 1, 2022.

The heaviest snow totals were measured along the east slopes of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and adjacent highlands due to the upslope flow from the backdoor cold front. EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several CoCoRaHS observers across the area measured new snow totals over six inches. The highest totals were received near Edgewood and Tijeras where measurements ranged from 6.0 to 6.3 inches of new snow.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow across the Santa Fe area resulted in snow packed and icy roadways that made for difficult driving conditions. In the early Wednesday morning hours near San Ildefonso Pueblo, two vehicles were involved in an automobile accident. Law enforcement responded to the crash near the intersection of New Mexico State Road 30 and New Mexico State Road 502. A man in one vehicle crossed the eastbound lane, striking a woman's vehicle which was traveling in the opposite direction. The man was pronounced dead at the scene while the woman was transported to a hospital. The condition of the woman is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level winter storm system tracked southward quickly through the Rockies and then through New Mexico on January 27, 2022. Meanwhile, an associated backdoor cold front also swept through eastern New Mexico ahead of this system. This combination resulted in a quick round of snow to northern and central New Mexico. As this storm system moved through the area quickly, snow totals were generally light with totals below 5 inches. The exception was across the Santa Fe metro area where a heavy band of snow resulted in snow totals of about 6 inches. Impacts: difficult driving conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several CoCoRaHS observers in Santa Fe measured six inches of snow that accumulated quickly during the late evening. This resulted in difficult driving conditions across the metro area.

CZ_NAME: JOHNSON AND BARTLETT MESAS INCLUDING RATON PASS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A very active weather pattern continued through October 2022 with several widespread rainfall events leading to near-record wet conditions in some areas for the month of October. Significant improvements continued for drought across the state, especially northern, central, and western NM. Widespread precipitation totals between two and five inches occurred across the southern half of the state with many areas across the northern half averaging one-half inch to one inch for the month. These totals were on the order of 200 to 400 percent of normal. By months end, only 21 percent of the state was in severe to exceptional drought, of which about 7 percent was extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from September 2022 continued through October.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early onset to the Southwest Monsoon brought above normal precipitation to the area, especially mountain locations where some areas received over 2 inches more precipitation than normal. Exceptional(D4) drought conditions were removed from all areas of southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: June started with the western half of the zone in Exceptional(D4) drought and the east in Extreme(D3) drought. By the end of the month, the west was in Extreme(D3) drought and the east in Severe(D2) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another month where all sites reported below normal precipitation kept southwest New Mexico in Severe(D2) to Extreme(D3) drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone remained in Severe(D2) drought through January.  The Cooperative Observer at Buckhorn reported 0.62 inches of precipitation which was 0.57 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving upper-level low pressure system with abundant moisture interacted with a backdoor cold front sliding southwest through eastern NM to produce widespread showers and isolated thunderstorms over NM during early October. Just enough instability and shear below the larger scale rotation aloft allowed a couple storms to produce brief funnel clouds over the region. A funnel cloud was observed near Edgewood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Forest Service employee spotted a funnel cloud near Edgewood around 445pm MDT on October 4, 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system deepened over the Rockies before tracking southward into New Mexico on February 1, 2022 and continuing into February 2nd. Accompanying this system was a backdoor cold front that brought in very cold arctic air into northern and central New Mexico. This blast of cold air combined with the slow moving system set the stage for widespread snow and winter weather impacts. The highest snow totals were measured across the northern high terrain of New Mexico where numerous SNOTELs and ski sites received two to three feet of new snow. Lower elevations also received heavy snow totals with with four to six inches common across the northeast and east central plains. Portions of the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas also received snow totals near eight inches. While the heavy snow resulted in severe travel conditions across most of northern and central New Mexico, these already poor conditions were exacerbated by the very cold temperatures from the arctic airmass. Snow and any water on the roadways were quickly frozen, leading to extremely icy conditions that lasted into February 4th before temperatures finally warmed above freezing. Across the Albuquerque area, numerous automobile accidents were reported with some injuries as well. Unfortunately, this storm system was indirectly responsible for four fatalities in automobile accidents. Two occurred at Sandia Peak, one occurred in Los Lunas, and the fourth occurred near Santa Rosa. Impacts: extreme cold, heavy snow, icy road conditions, poor travel, indirect fatalities, indirect injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer near La Madera measured 11.4 inches of snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching from the west starting February 15, 2022 and finally tracking over New Mexico on February 17th steered a potent upper-level jet overhead. Strengthening flow aloft, especially across southern New Mexico where the core of the jet was located, combined with deep mixing heights allowed for increasing surface winds. These winds first increased across the Sacramento Mountains on February 15th before becoming more widespread across areas south of Interstate 40 and east of Interstate 25 on February 16th. Wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph were most common across this area, however, high wind speeds were measured in several locations. The strongest wind gust of this event was recorded at Tucumcari where a brief 79 mph gust was measured. As the system finally tracked over the area on February 17th, it brought a round of light to moderate snow to portions of the area. The highest accumulations were measured across the northern mountains which received totals ranging from 3 to 8 inches. Lesser amounts were measured across central valleys, including at Albuquerque where snow totals across the metro ranged from about 1 to 3 inches. Impacts: high winds, heavy snow, difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station near Ruidoso measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an active end to January 2022, the weather pattern remained active for New Mexico throughout the month of February. However, despite multiple storm systems, the active pattern was once again characterized more by high winds and Arctic airmasses rather than precipitation. Despite northern and western mountains receiving snow totals over one to two feet throughout the month, poor snow water equivalent values continued which only aided in worsening drought conditions. Exceptional drought conditions, which had returned in January, spread into Chaves County during February. Drought conditions across the state worsened with about 80 percent in at least severe drought conditions. About 32 percent of that was categorized as extreme or exceptional drought with about 3 percent in exceptional drought. Precipitation totals across much of northern and central New Mexico generally ranged from one quarter to one half inch. High terrain areas across north central and western New Mexico fared better with precipitation totals closer to one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from January 2022 continued in February.

He was pronounced dead at the scene while the other six people had varying injuries and were transported to a hospital. Their conditions are unknown. No other injuries were reported.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system deepened over the Rockies before tracking southward into New Mexico on February 1, 2022 and continuing into February 2nd. Accompanying this system was a backdoor cold front that brought in very cold arctic air into northern and central New Mexico. This blast of cold air combined with the slow moving system set the stage for widespread snow and winter weather impacts. The highest snow totals were measured across the northern high terrain of New Mexico where numerous SNOTELs and ski sites received two to three feet of new snow. Lower elevations also received heavy snow totals with with four to six inches common across the northeast and east central plains. Portions of the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas also received snow totals near eight inches. While the heavy snow resulted in severe travel conditions across most of northern and central New Mexico, these already poor conditions were exacerbated by the very cold temperatures from the arctic airmass. Snow and any water on the roadways were quickly frozen, leading to extremely icy conditions that lasted into February 4th before temperatures finally warmed above freezing. Across the Albuquerque area, numerous automobile accidents were reported with some injuries as well. Unfortunately, this storm system was indirectly responsible for four fatalities in automobile accidents. Two occurred at Sandia Peak, one occurred in Los Lunas, and the fourth occurred near Santa Rosa. Impacts: extreme cold, heavy snow, icy road conditions, poor travel, indirect fatalities, indirect injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous sources reported heavy snow across the Albuquerque metro area. Snow totals varied across the city with the highest totals located in the northern half of the metro where a CoCoRaHS observer in Rio Rancho measured 7.5 inches of snow. At the official climate site at the Albuquerque International Airport, the snow total for this event was 1.6 inches. This snow combined with dangerously cold temperatures resulted in treacherous road conditions. The Albuquerque Police Department responded to 51 vehicle accidents during this event with three of those involving injuries. One indirect fatality also resulted from this event. A Rio Grande High School senior was struck by an out-of-control pickup truck in Los Lunas as he was helping a woman whose car had gotten stuck in the ice and snow. The 18-year-old male was pronounced dead at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow moving Pacific storm system brought a multitude of hazards to northern and central New Mexico from February 22, 2022 through February 24th. Ahead of this system, southwest flow aloft strengthened as this system steered a potent jet overhead. With deep mixing heights, these winds mixed down to the surface each day with gusts over 40 mph common areawide. With the core of this southwest-to-northeast oriented jet moving across the state on February 23th, surface winds peaked accordingly, especially across southern New Mexico. Wind gusts in excess of 50 mph were common with a couple sites in the lower Rio Grande Valley measuring high wind speeds over 58 mph. With dry conditions still in place, these winds resulted in areas of blowing dust and reduced visibility. The Sandia and Manzano Mountains, usually easily visible throughout the Albuquerque metro area, were obscured during the afternoon due to the blowing dust.||Meanwhile, an Arctic airmass surged into eastern and central New Mexico on February 22nd and 23rd, resulting in cold temperatures and setting the stage for snow across portions of the area. As this Pacific storm system dived southward closer to the Four Corners area, moisture ahead of it seeped into western New Mexico. This generated light snow shower activity starting late on February 21st, but snow showers peaked in coverage and intensity late on February 23rd as this system finally arrived to the Four Corners area. By the time snow tapered off during the morning of February 24th, northern and western mountains had received snow totals near a foot while lower elevations across the west received totals ranging from two to four inches. The Albuquerque and Santa Fe metro areas even picked up light snow accumulations of less than three inches. The combination of snow and cold temperatures from the Arctic airmass resulted in widespread difficult to severe travel conditions across western and central New Mexico. Impacts: high wind, blowing dust, strong crosswinds, heavy snow, icy roads.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A RAWS station near Old Horse Springs measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an active end to January 2022, the weather pattern remained active for New Mexico throughout the month of February. However, despite multiple storm systems, the active pattern was once again characterized more by high winds and Arctic airmasses rather than precipitation. Despite northern and western mountains receiving snow totals over one to two feet throughout the month, poor snow water equivalent values continued which only aided in worsening drought conditions. Exceptional drought conditions, which had returned in January, spread into Chaves County during February. Drought conditions across the state worsened with about 80 percent in at least severe drought conditions. About 32 percent of that was categorized as extreme or exceptional drought with about 3 percent in exceptional drought. Precipitation totals across much of northern and central New Mexico generally ranged from one quarter to one half inch. High terrain areas across north central and western New Mexico fared better with precipitation totals closer to one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from January 2022 continued in February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system deepened over the Rockies before tracking southward into New Mexico on February 1, 2022 and continuing into February 2nd. Accompanying this system was a backdoor cold front that brought in very cold arctic air into northern and central New Mexico. This blast of cold air combined with the slow moving system set the stage for widespread snow and winter weather impacts. The highest snow totals were measured across the northern high terrain of New Mexico where numerous SNOTELs and ski sites received two to three feet of new snow. Lower elevations also received heavy snow totals with with four to six inches common across the northeast and east central plains. Portions of the Santa Fe and Albuquerque metro areas also received snow totals near eight inches. While the heavy snow resulted in severe travel conditions across most of northern and central New Mexico, these already poor conditions were exacerbated by the very cold temperatures from the arctic airmass. Snow and any water on the roadways were quickly frozen, leading to extremely icy conditions that lasted into February 4th before temperatures finally warmed above freezing. Across the Albuquerque area, numerous automobile accidents were reported with some injuries as well. Unfortunately, this storm system was indirectly responsible for four fatalities in automobile accidents. Two occurred at Sandia Peak, one occurred in Los Lunas, and the fourth occurred near Santa Rosa. Impacts: extreme cold, heavy snow, icy road conditions, poor travel, indirect fatalities, indirect injuries.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Various SNOTELs across the Tusas Mountains measured snow totals in excess of ten inches. The highest snow totals in the area were near Canon Plaza where a SNOTEL received an estimated 21 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On February 21, 2022, a strong Pacific Northwest storm system was deepening over the Washington State and Oregon area. As a result of this, southwest flow aloft strengthened across the desert southwest, including New Mexico, as this system steered a strong jet overhead. Additionally, a lee-side surface low deepened over southeastern Colorado and northeastern New Mexico, and with deep mixing heights in place across the area, surface winds were windy areawide during the afternoon and early evening hours. Most of these winds ranged from 45 to 55 mph, especially across eastern New Mexico under the influence of the deepening surface low. However, the strongest wind gust recorded was at Gallup where a brief 59 mph non-thunderstorm gust was measured.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station in Gallup measured a peak wind speed of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system approaching from the west starting February 15, 2022 and finally tracking over New Mexico on February 17th steered a potent upper-level jet overhead. Strengthening flow aloft, especially across southern New Mexico where the core of the jet was located, combined with deep mixing heights allowed for increasing surface winds. These winds first increased across the Sacramento Mountains on February 15th before becoming more widespread across areas south of Interstate 40 and east of Interstate 25 on February 16th. Wind gusts of 40 to 55 mph were most common across this area, however, high wind speeds were measured in several locations. The strongest wind gust of this event was recorded at Tucumcari where a brief 79 mph gust was measured. As the system finally tracked over the area on February 17th, it brought a round of light to moderate snow to portions of the area. The highest accumulations were measured across the northern mountains which received totals ranging from 3 to 8 inches. Lesser amounts were measured across central valleys, including at Albuquerque where snow totals across the metro ranged from about 1 to 3 inches. Impacts: high winds, heavy snow, difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A White Sands Missile Range mesonet station southeast of Bosque del Apache measured a 59 mph wind gust.

As the system finally tracked over the area on February 17th, it brought a round of light to moderate snow to portions of the area. The highest accumulations were measured across the northern mountains which received totals ranging from 3 to 8 inches. Lesser amounts were measured across central valleys, including at Albuquerque where snow totals across the metro ranged from about 1 to 3 inches. Impacts: high winds, heavy snow, difficult travel conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A White Sands Missile Range mesonet station near Oscuro measured a 63 mph wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After an active end to January 2022, the weather pattern remained active for New Mexico throughout the month of February. However, despite multiple storm systems, the active pattern was once again characterized more by high winds and Arctic airmasses rather than precipitation. Despite northern and western mountains receiving snow totals over one to two feet throughout the month, poor snow water equivalent values continued which only aided in worsening drought conditions. Exceptional drought conditions, which had returned in January, spread into Chaves County during February. Drought conditions across the state worsened with about 80 percent in at least severe drought conditions. About 32 percent of that was categorized as extreme or exceptional drought with about 3 percent in exceptional drought. Precipitation totals across much of northern and central New Mexico generally ranged from one quarter to one half inch. High terrain areas across north central and western New Mexico fared better with precipitation totals closer to one inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from January 2022 continued in February.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deepening Southern Rockies trough caused westerly high winds across the Guadalupe, Delaware, and Davis Mountains.

With several weeks of persistent high winds and very dry conditions across northern and central New Mexico, early May brought more of the same to the area which was suffering from devastating wildfires. A very active storm track continued to affect the western United States, but instead of precipitation, high winds were much more common for New Mexico. Beginning on May 8, 2022, a slow-moving storm system began to track southward along the west coast which steered a potent polar jet across New Mexico. Wind speeds first strengthened across the northern high terrain early on May 8th and then spread into lower elevations during the afternoon and evening hours. These high winds persisted through the overnight hours and during much of the day on May 9th before finally diminishing after sunset. Wind speeds of 40 to 55 mph were common across much of northern and central New Mexico. High winds in excess of 58 mph were common across much of the high terrain and mountainous areas. This was particularly devasting for the ongoing wildfires across northern New Mexico. This dangerous pattern allowed the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon fires to spread at additional 20,000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Tucumcari measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Return flow set up across eastern New Mexico on May 23, 2022, helping to usher in beneficial low-level moisture. As the dryline sharpened across the eastern plains, a destabilizing atmosphere led to developing thunderstorms by mid-afternoon. As these thunderstorms tracked through the eastern plains, they intensified and became severe along the New Mexico and Texas border. The main hazard with these storms was hail, particularly in Curry and Roosevelt counties where multiple spotters reported at least one inch in diameter hail. Heavy rain was also common with these thunderstorms as rain totals were around one to two inches. With such a dry and parched surface though, only minor street ponding in Clovis was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter east-northeast of Portair reported quarter size hail from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level trough was over New Mexico and far West Texas. A cold front, along with outflow boundaries from storms to the north, moved into Southeast New Mexico and West Texas which further enhanced lift. Wind shear and instability were also high across the area. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced strong winds and large hail across parts of Southeast New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front moved through the eastern plains overnight on May 23, 2022, bringing impressive cold air advection into much of the plains. This ultimately reduced the severe weather threat on May 24th with less instability. However, modest instability managed to remain in place across southeastern New Mexico. As a low deepened over northeastern New Mexico, the polar jet dug into the state as well. With strong northwesterly flow aloft combined with moist easterly flow at the surface, this helped increase wind shear. Thus, the severe weather threat was confined to the southeastern plains during the afternoon, and a few thunderstorms produced large hail and strong winds across De Baca and Chaves counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portable RAWS station south-southeast of Elkins measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low-level southeasterly return flow set up across eastern New Mexico again on May 1, 2022. With the dryline developed north-to-south across the plains, this helped to set the stage for the development of afternoon thunderstorms. As the atmosphere destabilized, thunderstorms developed on the dryline before propagating northeast through the evening hours. While these thunderstorms prompted several Severe Thunderstorm Warnings, the only report received was penny-sized hail near Ragland.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer near Ragland reported 0.75 inch hail as a strong thunderstorm tracked through the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a storm system tracked eastward across Colorado on May 2, 2022, westerly flow across New Mexico strengthened in response. This increasing westerly flow quickly scoured out low-level moisture that had moved into eastern New Mexico the previous day, resulting in widespread very dry conditions. With the strongest winds aloft over the northern and central high terrain, the strongest westerly winds were measured in this area. A portable RAWS station in place for the ongoing Calf Canyon Fire in the Sangre de Cristo Mountains measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph while Clines Corners measured a wind gust of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portable RAWS station east southeast of Cowles measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Black Fire started the evening of May 13th north of Mimbres and by the end of the month would already be the 2nd largest wildfire in New Mexico history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The human started Black Fire started 24 miles north of Mimbres in Grant County and would spread through the month across the Blacks Range and into western Sierra County. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved through eastern and central New Mexico on May 15, 2022, helped to usher in better low-level moisture compared to the exceptionally dry conditions the area had been used to for several weeks. Meanwhile, a shortwave trough tracked eastward across Arizona, helping to set the stage for afternoon thunderstorms across eastern New Mexico on May 16, 2022. As the atmosphere destabilized, elevated thunderstorms developed across the central high terrain before advancing eastward. With these elevated thunderstorms, severe winds were the primary threat, and reports of 60 mph thunderstorm wind gusts were received from both Raton and Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station in Raton measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved through eastern and central New Mexico on May 15, 2022, helped to usher in better low-level moisture compared to the exceptionally dry conditions the area had been used to for several weeks. Meanwhile, a shortwave trough tracked eastward across Arizona, helping to set the stage for afternoon thunderstorms across eastern New Mexico on May 16, 2022. As the atmosphere destabilized, elevated thunderstorms developed across the central high terrain before advancing eastward. With these elevated thunderstorms, severe winds were the primary threat, and reports of 60 mph thunderstorm wind gusts were received from both Raton and Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station in Clayton measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an active storm track across the western United States continued, yet another upper-level trough tracked eastward across the central Rockies and to the north of New Mexico on May 20, 2022. In response, a lee-side surface low deepened across northeast New Mexico. This resulted in widespread breezy to windy conditions across northern and central New Mexico. Wind speeds were generally below 50 mph, but both Gallup and Santa Fe recorded brief high wind hits during the afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Gallup measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong backdoor cold front moved through eastern New Mexico and into the middle Rio Grande Valley on May 20, 2022. However, as westerly winds strengthened during the afternoon, this front retreated back towards the east. It was reinforced the next day, and as it pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain again, wind speeds strengthened significantly. A strong east canyon wind was felt across the middle Rio Grande Valley with wind speeds over of 45 to 55 mph generally. The strongest winds were felt on the east side of the Albuquerque metro area. Wind speeds were measured to be 58 mph late on May 21st, and minor reports of damage was also received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station at the Albuquerque International Airport measured a peak wind gust of 58 mph. This resulted in minor reports of damage across eastern portions of the city. A gas station canopy was reported to be toppled at the intersection of Carlisle and Candelaria. Tree limbs of four to five inches in diameter were also reported downed. Damages are a rough estimate.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cooks Peak Fire started north of Ocate on April 17, 2022. The wildfire continued to burn during the month of May, but fortunately, firefighters were able to fully contain the Cooks Peak Fire on May 13, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cooks Peak Fire started on April 17, 2022 on private land north of Ocate. As a persistent windy pattern continued through the month of April, the fire grew rapidly to 59,063 acres. At the start of the month of May, Philmont Scout Ranch Back Country west of New Mexico State Road 21 in Colax County were given mandatory evacuation orders while in Mora County, residents of Los LeFebres, Los Huerros, and residents east of County Road 009 and north of New Mexico Highway 120 were also given evacuation orders. Firefighters were able to make significant progress on the Cooks Peak Fire during the early part of May. By May 3rd, the wildfire was 59,359 acres with 72 percent containment. By May 13th, the wildfire was 100 percent contained with that same acreage. A total of four buildings were destroyed by the Cooks Peak Fire. Ten injuries were reported from firefighting efforts. Damage estimates were received from the State of New Mexico.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level low pressure system moved across Nevada and Utah. Very moist air was across most of West Texas and Southeast New Mexico. High instability and wind shear were also present. An upper level disturbance had moved over the area which provided additional lift. During the afternoon and evening hours, numerous severe thunderstorms developed across Southeast New Mexico and the Trans Pecos and moved across the Stockton Plateau and the Permian Basin. These storms produced large hail across Southeast New Mexico.

During a high wind event on April 12, 2022, a downed power line 4.3 miles southwest of Nogal sparked the Nogal Canyon Fire. This wildfire spread quickly under high winds and destroyed six homes and eight outbuildings. Firefighters were able to make quick progress on the wildfire throughout the remainder of April, and the Nogal Canyon Fire was 100 percent contained on May 2, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Nogal Canyon Fire was started on April 12, 2022 4.3 miles southwest of Nogal due to a downed power line. Fortunately, firefighters were able to fully contain the wildfire on May 2, 2022 with 433 acres burned. While six homes and eight outbuildings were destroyed in April when the fire started, no structures were destroyed in May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With several weeks of persistent high winds and very dry conditions across northern and central New Mexico, early May brought more of the same to the area which was suffering from devastating wildfires. A very active storm track continued to affect the western United States, but instead of precipitation, high winds were much more common for New Mexico. Beginning on May 8, 2022, a slow-moving storm system began to track southward along the west coast which steered a potent polar jet across New Mexico. Wind speeds first strengthened across the northern high terrain early on May 8th and then spread into lower elevations during the afternoon and evening hours. These high winds persisted through the overnight hours and during much of the day on May 9th before finally diminishing after sunset. Wind speeds of 40 to 55 mph were common across much of northern and central New Mexico. High winds in excess of 58 mph were common across much of the high terrain and mountainous areas. This was particularly devasting for the ongoing wildfires across northern New Mexico. This dangerous pattern allowed the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon fires to spread at additional 20,000 acres.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near Vanderwagen measured a peak sustained wind speed of 42 mph.

The McBride Fire started within the Village of Ruidoso on April 12, 2022, and quickly spread to over 6,000 acres. This fire was devastating to the area as it burned over 200 structures and was responsible for two fatalities. By the end of April 2022, the McBride Fire was 95 percent contained. On May 9, 2022, firefighters reached 100 percent containment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The McBride Fire was started on April 12, 2022 in Gavilan Canyon within the Village of Ruidoso. Due to a very persistent and windy pattern, this fire spread quickly to over 6,000 acres within five days. Firefighters fully contained the McBride Fire on May 9, 2022. While over 200 homes and structures were lost during April and two fatalities were confirmed, there are no known structures that were lost during May.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As yet another storm system began to track southward along the west coast of the United States on May 10, 2022, southwesterly flow strengthened across much of New Mexico. With the jet oriented southwest to northeast across southern California and Nevada, this spared New Mexico the strongest winds. Still, with the help of a lee-side surface low over northeastern New Mexico, strong and gusty winds were still common across all of northern and central New Mexico. Most of these gusts were below 50 mph, but Raton did measure a brief wind gust of 64 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Raton measured a peak wind gust of 64 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an upper-level ridge built over New Mexico on May 27, 2022, mid-level moisture seeping into the area underneath this ridge resulted in high based shower activity developing during the afternoon and evening hours. With very high mixing heights and dry surface conditions, any precipitation that fell out of these showers mainly evaporated and produced virga and strong dry microburst winds. Both the Albuquerque and Clines Corners ASOS stations measured downburst wind gusts in excess of 58 mph. Meanwhile, a dust devil was spotted in southwest Albuquerque, but it did no damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station at the Albuquerque Internation Airport measured a 67 mph wind gust associated with a very strong downburst.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An early onset to the Southwest Monsoon brought above normal precipitation to the area, especially mountain locations where some areas received over 2 inches more precipitation than normal. Exceptional(D4) drought conditions were removed from all areas of southern New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: June started with the eastern half of the zone in Exceptional(D4) drought with Severe(D2) drought over the far west.  By the end of the month, the east was in Extreme(D3) drought and the far west was in Moderate(D1) drought. The White Signal Cooperative Observer reported 2.85 inches of precipitation which was 2.27 inches above normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low pressure system from the Gulf of Alaska moved inland into the Pacific Northwest and tracked eastward over the northern Rockies on April 5, 2022. As this system tracked to the north of New Mexico, it steered a potent jet overhead which strengthened surface winds. Wind speeds of 45 to 55 mph where common across northern and central New Mexico with a few locations measuring high wind speeds. The highest gusts were measured at Clines Corners which measured a gust of 64 mph along with sustained winds of 47 mph. The windy conditions persisted on April 6, 2022 across northeast New Mexico. Unfortunately, these winds allowed a prescribed burn northwest of Las Vegas to get out of control and spread rapidly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station near Los Alamos measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station near Cimarron measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low-level southeasterly return flow setting up across eastern New Mexico allowed the dryline to develop across the plains on April 27, 2022. Increasing moisture along with an unstable atmosphere and an ample amount of wind shear allowed for the development of afternoon thunderstorms along the dryline. A few of these thunderstorms did become strong to severe. A trained spotter near Clayton reported severe winds while spotters in Quay County reported one inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near Clayton estimated wind gusts of 60 mph with a severe thunderstorm. These winds dropped visibility briefly down to one quarter mile with blowing dust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An amateur radio user near Ute Lake State Park reported one inch hail with a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptionally dry and windy conditions felt across northern and central New Mexico during the month of March only continued and even worsened during April. While the storm track remained active across the western United States, it wasn't favorable to New Mexico as these systems largely remained to the north of the state. As such, precipitation was scarce during April with amounts well below one inch. The Albuquerque area recorded only a trace of precipitation during the month, well below the normal of 0.51 inches. As these dry, windy conditions persisted, this only fueled numerous wildfire starts across northern and central New Mexico, and drought conditions only worsened with about 96 percent of the state in at least severe drought conditions. Areas of extreme to exceptional drought spread rapidly with now about 68 percent of the state in these categories, and about 16 percent of that in exceptional drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from March 2022 continued in April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a storm system tracked to the north of New Mexico on April 11-12, 2022, it steered a potent polar jet over the state. With a continued deep surface low pressure system over northeastern New Mexico and ample deep mixing across all of northern and central New Mexico, windy conditions persisted. Winds peaked on April 12th as the core of the jet crossed the state, and many areas measured wind gusts in excess of 58 mph. The strongest winds during this event were recorded along and east of the central mountain chain which was in closer proximity to the deep surface low. Ruidoso and areas nearby were hardest hit with several hours of wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph. With exceptionally dry conditions also present, this fueled the start for the McBride Fire which prompted evacuation orders and was responsible for the deaths of two citizens. These conditions also helped to start the Big Hole Fire near Belen as well as the Nogal Canyon Fire near Capitan, which threatened multiple homes and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station near Santa Rosa measured a peak wind gust of 69 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a very strong low pressure system tracked into California on April 22, 2022, it steered an incredibly potent upper-level jet across New Mexico. Winds at 250mb within this jet reached 90 to 100 knots while at 700mb, these westerly winds strengthened to 50 to 60 knots. Meanwhile, deep atmospheric mixing along with a deepening surface low over northern Colorado only helped to enhance surface wind speeds. This set the stage for a widespread damaging wind event across all of northern and central New Mexico. 60 to 70 mph winds were common for several hours across all of northern and central New Mexico with higher gusts of 80 to 95 mph measured across the northern high terrain and northeast highlands. These strong winds created extensive areas of blowing dust, especially across western and central New Mexico. Numerous sites, such as Farmington, Gallup, Santa Fe, and Albuquerque, reported reduced visibility to less than one quarter mile at times. This caused poor travel conditions, and major highways like Interstate 25 near Santa Fe were forced to close due to several traffic accidents. With exceptionally dry conditions persisting, this set the stage for a catastrophic fire weather day with with at least twenty new fire starts. The ongoing Cooks Peak, Hermits Peak, and Calf Canyon wildfires experienced exceptional growth underneath these high winds and exploded in size within hours. The Calf Canyon Fire threatened numerous communities across Mora and San Miguel counties, prompting numerous evacuations for many residents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A New Mexico Department of Transportation weather station at Interstate 40 and New Mexico State Road 6 measured a peak sustained wind speed of 48 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low-level southeasterly return flow setting up across eastern New Mexico allowed the dryline to develop across the plains on April 27, 2022. Increasing moisture along with an unstable atmosphere and an ample amount of wind shear allowed for the development of afternoon thunderstorms along the dryline. A few of these thunderstorms did become strong to severe. A trained spotter near Clayton reported severe winds while spotters in Quay County reported one inch hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near Logan reported 1 inch hail from a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As has been all too common during the month of April, a storm system tracking eastward through the central Rockies remained too far north for any precipitation for New Mexico. Instead, this system increased westerly winds aloft on April 29th which in turn, strengthened surface winds during the afternoon and evening hours. With deep atmospheric mixing and the presence of a lee-side surface low, westerly surface winds were quick to increase by late morning before peaking during the afternoon hours. The strongest winds were measured across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains where gusts in excess of 60 mph were common. Unfortunately, ongoing wildfires only fed off these strong winds. The combined Calf Canyon and Hermits Peak Wildfire spread rapidly towards the southeast during the afternoon, prompting mandatory evacuations for portions of western San Miguel County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A portable RAWS station east-southeast of Cowles measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptionally dry and windy conditions felt across northern and central New Mexico during the month of March only continued and even worsened during April. While the storm track remained active across the western United States, it wasn't favorable to New Mexico as these systems largely remained to the north of the state. As such, precipitation was scarce during April with amounts well below one inch. The Albuquerque area recorded only a trace of precipitation during the month, well below the normal of 0.51 inches. As these dry, windy conditions persisted, this only fueled numerous wildfire starts across northern and central New Mexico, and drought conditions only worsened with about 96 percent of the state in at least severe drought conditions. Areas of extreme to exceptional drought spread rapidly with now about 68 percent of the state in these categories, and about 16 percent of that in exceptional drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from March 2022 worsened in April with extreme drought conditions developing across eastern portions of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A closed upper-level low pressure system from the Gulf of Alaska moved inland into the Pacific Northwest and tracked eastward over the northern Rockies on April 5, 2022. As this system tracked to the north of New Mexico, it steered a potent jet overhead which strengthened surface winds. Wind speeds of 45 to 55 mph where common across northern and central New Mexico with a few locations measuring high wind speeds. The highest gusts were measured at Clines Corners which measured a gust of 64 mph along with sustained winds of 47 mph. The windy conditions persisted on April 6, 2022 across northeast New Mexico. Unfortunately, these winds allowed a prescribed burn northwest of Las Vegas to get out of control and spread rapidly.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On April 6, 2022, a prescribed burn was started 12 miles northwest of Las Vegas, but due to windy conditions in place, the fire soon got out of control and spread rapidly. By April 13th, the Hermits Peak Fire grew to 7,900 acres with 10 percent containment. On April 22nd, the wildfire experienced rapid growth as a historic and catastrophic fire weather pattern developed across New Mexico. Despite 91 percent containment, the wildfire experienced rapid growth and combined with the nearby Calf Canyon Fire. Together, these two wildfires burned over 42,000 acres. As very windy conditions persisted through the remainder of April, the Hermits Peak Fire continued to spread rapidly, and by the end of the month on April 30th, the combined acreage between it and the Calf Canyon Fire was 103,908 acres with now 30 percent containment. Currently, the wildfire may not be fully contained until July 2022. Seven outbuildings were destroyed by this wildfire with an additional 600 structures threatened. Mandatory evacuations were not ordered. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a storm system tracked to the north of New Mexico on April 11-12, 2022, it steered a potent polar jet over the state. With a continued deep surface low pressure system over northeastern New Mexico and ample deep mixing across all of northern and central New Mexico, windy conditions persisted. Winds peaked on April 12th as the core of the jet crossed the state, and many areas measured wind gusts in excess of 58 mph. The strongest winds during this event were recorded along and east of the central mountain chain which was in closer proximity to the deep surface low. Ruidoso and areas nearby were hardest hit with several hours of wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph. With exceptionally dry conditions also present, this fueled the start for the McBride Fire which prompted evacuation orders and was responsible for the deaths of two citizens. These conditions also helped to start the Big Hole Fire near Belen as well as the Nogal Canyon Fire near Capitan, which threatened multiple homes and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Gallup measured a peak wind gust of 62 mph.

The Big Hole Fire spread quickly to 890 acres by April 15th with 60 percent containment, and the wildfire was finally 100 percent contained on April 25th. One firefighter was injured while responding. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture remained over southern New Mexico as a weak upper trough was lifting out of southern Arizona.  Numerous showers and thunderstorms developed over the higher terrain of the Gila region which brought flash flooding to the Monticello area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several videos from a spotter as well as verbal confirmation of flash flooding near Highway 142.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptionally dry and windy conditions felt across northern and central New Mexico during the month of March only continued and even worsened during April. While the storm track remained active across the western United States, it wasn't favorable to New Mexico as these systems largely remained to the north of the state. As such, precipitation was scarce during April with amounts well below one inch. The Albuquerque area recorded only a trace of precipitation during the month, well below the normal of 0.51 inches. As these dry, windy conditions persisted, this only fueled numerous wildfire starts across northern and central New Mexico, and drought conditions only worsened with about 96 percent of the state in at least severe drought conditions. Areas of extreme to exceptional drought spread rapidly with now about 68 percent of the state in these categories, and about 16 percent of that in exceptional drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from March 2022 continued in April.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a storm system tracked to the north of New Mexico on April 11-12, 2022, it steered a potent polar jet over the state. With a continued deep surface low pressure system over northeastern New Mexico and ample deep mixing across all of northern and central New Mexico, windy conditions persisted. Winds peaked on April 12th as the core of the jet crossed the state, and many areas measured wind gusts in excess of 58 mph. The strongest winds during this event were recorded along and east of the central mountain chain which was in closer proximity to the deep surface low. Ruidoso and areas nearby were hardest hit with several hours of wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph. With exceptionally dry conditions also present, this fueled the start for the McBride Fire which prompted evacuation orders and was responsible for the deaths of two citizens. These conditions also helped to start the Big Hole Fire near Belen as well as the Nogal Canyon Fire near Capitan, which threatened multiple homes and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near Vallecitos measured a peak sustained wind speed of 41 mph.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A downed power line about 4.3 miles southwest of Nogal sparked the Nogal Canyon Fire on April 12, 2022. This fire spread to 400 acres by April 13th and destroyed 6 homes and 8 outbuildings while threatening 50 others. Residences in Nogal Canyon up to New Mexico State Highway 37 were given evacuation orders. By April 17th, the fire had spread to 433 acres with 4 percent containment. By the end of the month on April 30th, the Nogal Canyon Fire was 84 percent contained with full containment anticipated in May. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface high pressure developed to the east of New Mexico, it established easterly to southeasterly return flow across eastern New Mexico. However, on April 19th and April 20, 2022, a storm system tracking eastward across the central Rockies strengthened westerly flow aloft which quickly overtook the low-level return flow. This was aided by a lee-side surface low across northeastern New Mexico. With deep atmospheric mixing still in place across all of northern and central New Mexico, these stronger westerly winds mixed down to the surface. Most wind gusts across the area were generally between 40 and 50 mph, but wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were measured at Clines Corners, Gallup, and Angel Fire. Unfortunately, a persistent critical fire weather pattern helped to spark the Calf Canyon Fire in Gallinas Canyon on the Santa Fe National Forest along U.S. Highway 65 on April 19, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Clines Corners measured several hours of high wind speeds with a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a very strong low pressure system tracked into California on April 22, 2022, it steered an incredibly potent upper-level jet across New Mexico. Winds at 250mb within this jet reached 90 to 100 knots while at 700mb, these westerly winds strengthened to 50 to 60 knots. Meanwhile, deep atmospheric mixing along with a deepening surface low over northern Colorado only helped to enhance surface wind speeds. This set the stage for a widespread damaging wind event across all of northern and central New Mexico. 60 to 70 mph winds were common for several hours across all of northern and central New Mexico with higher gusts of 80 to 95 mph measured across the northern high terrain and northeast highlands. These strong winds created extensive areas of blowing dust, especially across western and central New Mexico. Numerous sites, such as Farmington, Gallup, Santa Fe, and Albuquerque, reported reduced visibility to less than one quarter mile at times. This caused poor travel conditions, and major highways like Interstate 25 near Santa Fe were forced to close due to several traffic accidents. With exceptionally dry conditions persisting, this set the stage for a catastrophic fire weather day with with at least twenty new fire starts. The ongoing Cooks Peak, Hermits Peak, and Calf Canyon wildfires experienced exceptional growth underneath these high winds and exploded in size within hours. The Calf Canyon Fire threatened numerous communities across Mora and San Miguel counties, prompting numerous evacuations for many residents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station near Los Alamos measured a peak sustained wind speed of 47 mph. Several hours of high wind speeds reduced visibility at Los Alamos to 1 to 2 statute miles from blowing dust.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported torrential downpour with a lot of fast moving running water near their residence.  The flood waters damaged a few of their fences.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several days of heavy rain continued across southwest New Mexico as a slow moving mid and upper level trough helped produce widespread rain across the region with nearly 5 inches of rain reported near White Sands. As a result of the heavy rain, numerous reports of flash flooding were received.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: New Mexico Highway 11 was closed from mile marker 68 to 86 due to flooded low water crossings.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: April 10, 2022 marked the beginning of a very windy pattern for all of northern and central New Mexico as multiple storm systems tracked across the Rockies. With the storm track to the north of New Mexico, this opened the door for strong upper-level winds to be steered across the state. With a persistent surface low pressure system and deep mixing also present, these winds were easily able to mix down to the surface. While westerly winds began to pick up on April 10th, most of the area measured winds of 40-50 mph. The exceptions were across the northeast and central highlands where wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were common. The highest wind gust recorded was at Raton which measured at 78 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Clines Corners measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface high pressure developed to the east of New Mexico, it established easterly to southeasterly return flow across eastern New Mexico. However, on April 19th and April 20, 2022, a storm system tracking eastward across the central Rockies strengthened westerly flow aloft which quickly overtook the low-level return flow. This was aided by a lee-side surface low across northeastern New Mexico. With deep atmospheric mixing still in place across all of northern and central New Mexico, these stronger westerly winds mixed down to the surface. Most wind gusts across the area were generally between 40 and 50 mph, but wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were measured at Clines Corners, Gallup, and Angel Fire. Unfortunately, a persistent critical fire weather pattern helped to spark the Calf Canyon Fire in Gallinas Canyon on the Santa Fe National Forest along U.S. Highway 65 on April 19, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station near Angel Fire measured a peak wind gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a very strong low pressure system tracked into California on April 22, 2022, it steered an incredibly potent upper-level jet across New Mexico. Winds at 250mb within this jet reached 90 to 100 knots while at 700mb, these westerly winds strengthened to 50 to 60 knots. Meanwhile, deep atmospheric mixing along with a deepening surface low over northern Colorado only helped to enhance surface wind speeds. This set the stage for a widespread damaging wind event across all of northern and central New Mexico. 60 to 70 mph winds were common for several hours across all of northern and central New Mexico with higher gusts of 80 to 95 mph measured across the northern high terrain and northeast highlands. These strong winds created extensive areas of blowing dust, especially across western and central New Mexico. Numerous sites, such as Farmington, Gallup, Santa Fe, and Albuquerque, reported reduced visibility to less than one quarter mile at times. This caused poor travel conditions, and major highways like Interstate 25 near Santa Fe were forced to close due to several traffic accidents. With exceptionally dry conditions persisting, this set the stage for a catastrophic fire weather day with with at least twenty new fire starts. The ongoing Cooks Peak, Hermits Peak, and Calf Canyon wildfires experienced exceptional growth underneath these high winds and exploded in size within hours. The Calf Canyon Fire threatened numerous communities across Mora and San Miguel counties, prompting numerous evacuations for many residents.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near Glorieta measured a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As exceptionally dry conditions persisted across northern and central New Mexico and most days during the month of April featured breezy to windy conditions, the stage was set for the rapid development of any fires that formed. When the Cooks Peak Fire was sparked north of Ocate on April 17, 2022, it rapidly spread throughout the month of April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: On April 17, 2022, the Cooks Peak Fire began on private land north of Ocate near the Mora and Colfax county line. The wildfire experienced its most rapid growth on April 22nd as a historic and catastrophic fire weather pattern set up over New Mexico underneath widespread 60 to 80 mph wind gusts. An infrared flight on the night of April 22nd estimated that the wildfire had grown to 42,672 acres. The rapid growth of this fire prompted evacuations for numerous communities across Mora and Colfax counties. The rapid growth of the wildfire continued through the remainder of the month with a persistent and dangerous windy pattern. By the end of the month on April 30th, the Cooks Peak Fire had burned 59,063 acres with 69 percent containment. Currently, full containment is expected in May 2022.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The exceptionally dry and windy conditions felt across northern and central New Mexico during the month of March only continued and even worsened during April. While the storm track remained active across the western United States, it wasn't favorable to New Mexico as these systems largely remained to the north of the state. As such, precipitation was scarce during April with amounts well below one inch. The Albuquerque area recorded only a trace of precipitation during the month, well below the normal of 0.51 inches. As these dry, windy conditions persisted, this only fueled numerous wildfire starts across northern and central New Mexico, and drought conditions only worsened with about 96 percent of the state in at least severe drought conditions. Areas of extreme to exceptional drought spread rapidly with now about 68 percent of the state in these categories, and about 16 percent of that in exceptional drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from March 2022 worsened in April as exceptional drought conditions began to develop across portions of the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a storm system tracked to the north of New Mexico on April 11-12, 2022, it steered a potent polar jet over the state. With a continued deep surface low pressure system over northeastern New Mexico and ample deep mixing across all of northern and central New Mexico, windy conditions persisted. Winds peaked on April 12th as the core of the jet crossed the state, and many areas measured wind gusts in excess of 58 mph. The strongest winds during this event were recorded along and east of the central mountain chain which was in closer proximity to the deep surface low. Ruidoso and areas nearby were hardest hit with several hours of wind gusts of 70 to 80 mph. With exceptionally dry conditions also present, this fueled the start for the McBride Fire which prompted evacuation orders and was responsible for the deaths of two citizens. These conditions also helped to start the Big Hole Fire near Belen as well as the Nogal Canyon Fire near Capitan, which threatened multiple homes and structures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A weather station near Estancia measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a surface high pressure developed to the east of New Mexico, it established easterly to southeasterly return flow across eastern New Mexico. However, on April 19th and April 20, 2022, a storm system tracking eastward across the central Rockies strengthened westerly flow aloft which quickly overtook the low-level return flow. This was aided by a lee-side surface low across northeastern New Mexico. With deep atmospheric mixing still in place across all of northern and central New Mexico, these stronger westerly winds mixed down to the surface. Most wind gusts across the area were generally between 40 and 50 mph, but wind gusts of 55 to 65 mph were measured at Clines Corners, Gallup, and Angel Fire. Unfortunately, a persistent critical fire weather pattern helped to spark the Calf Canyon Fire in Gallinas Canyon on the Santa Fe National Forest along U.S. Highway 65 on April 19, 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While daily rounds of widespread high fire danger persisted across northern and central New Mexico throughout the month of April, conditions were primed for rapid wildfire growth. The Calf Canyon Fire was sparked on April 19, 2022, near Gallinas Canyon. The wildfire experienced its most rapid growth on April 22nd as a historic and catastrophic fire weather pattern developed across New Mexico. With several hours of widespread 60 to 80 mph wind gusts, the Calf Canyon Fire exploded. This prompted the National Weather Service Albuquerque Office to issue a rare Fire Weather Warning for mandatory evacuations across western San Miguel and Mora counties. By the morning of April 23rd, it was reported that the Calf Canyon Fire had merged with the nearby Hermits Peak Fire, and together, these two fires had burned more than 42,000 acres. A week later, on April 29th, yet another widespread dangerous wind event caused the Calf Canyon and Hermits Peak fires to continue to rapidly spread. This prompted another set of mandatory evacuations across western San Miguel County and the issuance of another rare Fire Weather Warning. By the end of the month of April 30th, the Calf Canyon and Hermits Peak wildfires had burned a combined 103,908 acres with 30 percent containment. The Calf Canyon Fire may not be fully contained until July 2022. Up until this point of the fire season, the Calf Canyon Fire has been the most devastating for New Mexico. It is estimated that many homes and structures have been destroyed as citizens come forward with stories about losing everything they have. However, at this time, there is no estimate on how much has been lost as officials have been unable to do official surveys. Damages are estimated.
