EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain near Springer flooded U.S. Highway 56, making it impassable. A barn along the highway had 4 inches of standing water while a house and its backyard had 2 to 3 inches of standing water. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: While thunderstorm coverage was not as widespread compared to the previous day, July 21, 2022, was still a very active monsoon day for northern New Mexico in part due to the training of thunderstorms along the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar. Thunderstorms developed early in the day with steering flow tracking them slowly towards the southeast. Thunderstorms continuously developed along the Sangre de Cristo Mountains through the daytime hours, and thus, kept impacting the same areas. The Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar was hit particularly hard with rainfall estimated of at least two to four inches with locally higher amounts likely. Highway 518 near Holman and Cleveland, which had been hit hard the previous day, were hit hard again with significant flooding and debris flow observed. The southern portion of the burn scar also received significant rainfall totals, and unfortunately, this flooding took the lives of three individuals at Camp Blue Haven.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms across the southern portion of the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar in San Miguel County produced an estimated 2 to 4 inches of rainfall within the Tecolote Creek and Gallinas Creek areas which caused significant and catastropic flash flooding. Flash flooding and debris flow washed out many roadways within the area, rendering them impassable. The camp director at the Camp Blue Haven said that their playground was underwater for about 40 minutes until water receded. At 2:40 PM MDT, the chief of the Cabo Lucero Volunteer Fire Department received a call about a vehicle floating within the flood waters. When officials responded to the floating vehicle, they also discovered two people dead nearby. While initially thought to have been in their vehicle, officials later determined that the two women, 62-year-old Linda June Cummings and 84-year-old Betty Greenhaw, were swept away from a destroyed home within the Tecolote Canyon subdivision. A third individual from the same home was reported missing, and the National Guard, New Mexico State Police, and San Miguel County Sheriff's Office assised in search and rescue for several days. Finally on Tuesday, July 26th, they found the body of 62-year-old Jimmy Chris Cummings. All three were from Hale Center, Texas, but had a 100-year-lease on a cabin in Tecolote Canyon on the Camp Blue Haven property which they were visiting at the time. Eight feet of water rushing down into the canyon took down two walls of the cabin and partially destroyed the other two walls. Damages for this event are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An abundant amount of moisture in the atmosphere led to another very active monsoon day for northern and central New Mexico on July 29, 2022. Thunderstorms developed early in the day across the high terrain areas before becoming more widespread as the day progressed. Heavy rain was common with these thunderstorms, especially on the Calf Canyon burn scar which reported more burn scar flash flooding and debris flow. The Gallup area also received heavy rainfall which forced the closure of a few area roadways. Meanwhile, a few thunderstorms across the eastern plains briefly turned strong to severe which prompted a few Severe Thunderstorm Warnings. One thunderstorm near Logan looked like it was producing baseball size hail according to radar, but a trained spotter only reported quarter size hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on the Calf Canyon burn scar caused the Rito San Jose to overflow its banks and flood low water crossings along County Road A4 near Rociada. No damages were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture values continued to be on the rise across northern and central New Mexico on July 20, 2022, and this led to a very active monsoon day for the area. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms were common across much of the area, particularly across the mountainous terrain. New Mexico's recent burn scars continued to  be magnets for heavy thunderstorms with known impacts across the McBride burn scar as well as the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar. Meanwhile, thunderstorms also produced brief strong winds in a couple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on the northern portion of the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar caused significant flash flooding near Holman. Video obtained by KRQE showed fast moving water near residences in Holman, and this flooding also washed out Highway 518. Canoncito Creek overflowed again as well, creating deep water below homes and destroying a number of pastures. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As above normal moisture remained in place across northern and central New Mexico, a very active monsoon pattern persisted with numerous thunderstorms impacting the area on July 12, 2022. The Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar continued to be a magnet for heavy rainfall as an estimated 1.5 inches of rain resulted in flooding of roadways near Chacon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Manuelitas Creek rose four to five feet and overtopped two bridges along County Road A1 east of State Road 94 between Sapello and Manuelitas. One CoCoRaHS observer near Sapello measured 3.76 inches of rain and remarked that it was the highest flooding levels they had seen in their 58 years at that residence. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of July continued to be busy for meteorologists at the Albuquerque National Weather Service Office as numerous thunderstorms impacted the area on July 30, 2022. With well above normal moisture available in the atmosphere, these thunderstorms were robust and very capable of producing heavy rainfall. Several flash flooding reports were received, including in the Rio Rancho and Edgewood areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Rio Rancho and Vista Hills areas caused flooding. At a home in Rio Rancho, standing water was reported two inches deep in someone's backyard. In Vista Hills, dirt roads were flooded and arroyos were reported to be running. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon season got off to an early start, and that momentum continued throughout the entire month of July with yet another very busy and active day on July 31, 2022. Per usual, thunderstorms developed early in the day across the high terrain areas before becoming more widespread throughout the evening. Many of these thunderstorms were capable of producing heavy rain which resulted in flash flooding reports across the area. The Calf Canyon burn scar was hit again with flash flooding and debris flow near Gascon while flash flooding across western New Mexico forced the closure of a few roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near San Fidel reported flash flooding on San Jose Loop. No damages are known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a typical monsoon day across northern and central New Mexico on July 23, 2022, as an abundant amount of moisture led to scattered to numerous thunderstorms. The Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar proved once again to be the area of most concern as heavy rainfall on the northern end of the burn area led to flash flooding. The Holman and Cleveland areas, which have been hardest hit so far this summer, were hit hard again with flash flooding and debris flow.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon Fire, which were originally two separate wildfires that combined during the month of April 2022, continued during the month of July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At the end of June 2022, the largest wildfire in New Mexico State history had burned 341,735 acres with 93% containment. Containment on the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon Fire was anticipated in July, but the western edge of the wildfire was within very steep terrain that was inaccessible to first responders. Thus, progress on this wildfire slowed down during the month of July. Fortunately, a very active month for monsoon season brought much needed moisture to the fire, and containment rose to 96% with no change in the acreage burned. No known structures were lost during the month. Note: While originally known as the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon Fire, officials renamed the fire to the Calf Canyon Fire on July 26, 2022. Moving forward, this is how the wildfire will be referred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The North American Monsoon arrived early this year and stayed active throughout all of the month of July 2022 across northern and central New Mexico. Most of the month saw a traditional monsoon pattern develop which meant that the monsoonal moisture plume was focused primarily across western and central New Mexico. This allowed for daily rounds of thunderstorms to focus in these areas with eastern New Mexico commonly seeing more hot and dry days rather than rainy ones. Precipitation totals across western and central New Mexico were generally around 2 to 4 inches during the month with some locally higher amounts in the northern high terrain. Across the east, precipitation totals were generally less than one inch. The drought improvements noted in June continued throughout the month of July with now 76 percent of the state in at least severe drought. About 33 percent of that was in at least extreme drought with about 11 percent in the exceptional category. Most of the improvement was along and west of the central mountain chain with no improvement across the eastern plains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from June 2022 continued in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: It was a typical monsoon day across northern and central New Mexico on July 23, 2022, as an abundant amount of moisture led to scattered to numerous thunderstorms. The Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar proved once again to be the area of most concern as heavy rainfall on the northern end of the burn area led to flash flooding. The Holman and Cleveland areas, which have been hardest hit so far this summer, were hit hard again with flash flooding and debris flow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A heavy thunderstorm over the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar early in the day on July 23rd dropped between 0.50 and 0.75 inches of rain. This prompted a Flash Flood Warning for the burn scar, however, no reports of flooding were received. The water from this thunderstorm was held back thanks to debris flow. Around midnight, the New Mexico State EOC relayed reports of major flooding and debris flow from a light shower that was only producing a couple hundredths of an inch. This light rain was enough to release the heavier rain that had fallen earlier that day which washed out roads and impacted the Holman area. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An active moonsoon pattern continued across New Mexico on July 25, 2022, as a steady stream of moisture stayed in place over western and central New Mexico. Additionally, an easterly wave over northern Mexico that was trapped underneath the high pressure centered over the lower Mississippi Valley. An abundant amount of moisture in addition to this easterly wave resulted in robust thunderstorms across southern New Mexico that produced heavy rainfall. Socorro and Lincoln counties, including the McBride burn scar, were hit hardest with flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lincoln County emergency manager reported flash flooding and debris flow from the McBride burn scar at Gavilan Canyon and Warrior Drive. This forced the closure of the roadway. Local law enforcement remarked that this flooding was worse than the event on July 20th. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a deep plume of moisture focused across western and central New Mexico, another active monsoon day occurred on July 2, 2022. Thunderstorms first initiated along the mountainous terrain with steering flow tracking this activity in a northerly to northeasterly direction. Several thunderstorms that developed near the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar produced an estimated 0.75 to 1.25 inches along the northern half of the burn scar. That resulted in flash flooding and debris flow along Highway 94 near Ledoux.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Local law enforcement reported that heavy rain and debris flow made Highway 94 as well as County Road C 001 impassable which forced the closure of the roads. New Mexico Department of Transportation personnel worked through the overnight hours to clear the roadways and reopen them. Along Maestas Canyon, a lifelong resident was caught by surprise when four feet of water, mud, and debris came rushing down the canyon. A large shipping container also caught within the flood crashed into his cabin. The resident fled in his vehicle, and fortunately suffered no injuries. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With above normal amounts of atmospheric moisture and an easterly wave still in the area, the active monsoon pattern continued for portions of New Mexico on July 26, 2022. Thunderstorms developed early in the day and became more numerous throughout the afternoon and evening hours. The Calf Canyon burn scar was hit twice with heavy thunderstorms, but only reported rises along Gallinas Creek. Elsewhere, heavy rain caused flash flooding in San Juan County as well as near Glorieta and Tesuque. Note: The Calf Canyon burn scar had previously been referred to as the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar. On July 26, 2022, officials renamed it to the Calf Canyon burn scar, therefore, from here on out, this is how it will be referenced.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Flora Vista to Bloomfield area in San Juan County caused significant flash flooding in the area. A spotter reported that approximately two feet of water covered County Road 3150, rendering it impassable. Two vehicles were washed away and one home was flooded. Near Crouch Mesa, the San Juan emergency manager reported flash flooding over area roadways, and an RV was washed out. Damages are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from June 2022 continued in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent storm system moving eastward toward NM from the Great Basin produced widespread strong winds across central and eastern NM on November 3, 2022. Southwest winds increased over the high terrain during the early morning hours then spread to much of the region with gusts of 45 to 60 mph in many areas. The highest winds impacted the area from near Albuquerque to Clines Corners, Las Vegas, and Raton. Snow was reported with this system as well as much colder air moved into the region. High terrain areas of northern NM picked up their first moderate snowfall amounts of four to eight inches. Minor travel disruptions were reported through the high terrain.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clines Corners reported a peak wind gust up to 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A powerful jet stream focused across the central Rockies set the stage for strong mountain activity along the central mountain chain of NM on November 5, 2022. The strongest winds impacted the area from Clines Corners to Fort Sumner where gusts exceeded 60 to 70 mph at times. Hazardous travel conditions occurred along Interstate 40 and parts of U.S. Highway 285 however no damage or travel incidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Potent mountain wave activity produced an extended period of wind gusts well in excess of 58 mph at Clines Corners. Wind gusts greater than 70 mph occurred for around three hours with the highest gust recorded at 77 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving winter storm system moved east through northern New Mexico during the overnight hours of November 13-14, 2022. Several locations along the central mountain chain and nearby highlands picked up one to four inches of snowfall during the early morning hours as a potent cold front moved through the region. The heaviest snowfall occurred in the southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains where an intense burst of snow resulted in eight to 12 inches in less than eight hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several observation platforms including SNOTELs and ski sites reported snowfall accumulations between 8 and 12 inches in less than 10 hours from near Tres Ritos to Santa Fe Ski Basin.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November 2022 was much drier for most of the state with only a couple snowfall events across the northern high terrain. Very little improvement was made to drought conditions as most areas picked up less than one-quarter inch of precipitation for November. By months end, roughly 18 percent of the state was in severe to extreme drought, of which about 3 percent was extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Moderate to severe drought conditions persisted across the area through November 2022.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very active monsoon pattern over the past couple of months showed no signs of slowing during the month of September which contributed to the continued improvement in New Mexico drought conditions. The pattern during the month of September favored the highest precipitation totals over western and northern New Mexico which saw the greatest drought improvement this time. Eastern New Mexico remained relatively drier, and as such, saw little to no change in drought conditions in this area. Precipitation totals across western and central New Mexico were generally around 1 to 2 inches during the month while totals less than one inch were more common across the east. About 31 percent of the state was in at least severe drought. About 7 percent of that was in extreme drought with no exceptional drought in the state since the beginning of 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from August 2022 continued in September.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level storm system moved quickly east across NM with widespread strong west to northwest winds on November 28-29, 2022. The strongest winds impacted areas along the central mountain chain into the nearby highlands where gusts of 55 to 75 mph were reported. A massive snarl of tumbleweeds collected at the Santa Fe airport. Some very light snow also fell over the Tusas Mountains with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Magdalena Ridge Observatory reported several hours with peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 67 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though the last month of the monsoon season brought slightly below normal rainfall, late August precipitation helped to improve drought conditions across southern New Mexico in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone went from Severe(D2) drought to Moderate(D1) drought. The Hillsboro Cooperative Observer reported 1.45 inches of precipitation which was 0.52 inches below normal.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The southern half of the zone started the month in Extreme(D3) drought with Severe(D2) drought across the north. By the end of September, the entire zone was in Moderate(D1) drought.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The western half of the zone started out in Extreme(D3) drought improving to Moderate(D1) across the east.  By the end of September, the west was in Severe(D2) drought with Moderate(D1) drought remaining over the east.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though the last month of the monsoon season brought slightly below normal rainfall, late August precipitation helped to improve drought conditions across southern New Mexico in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: September started out with Severe(D2) drought over the northwest and improving to Abnormally Dry(D0) drought over the southeast.  By the end of September, just the far northwest remained in Severe(D2) drought with no drought conditions over the southeast. The New Mexico State University Cooperative Observer reported 0.86 inches which was 0.55 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though the last month of the monsoon season brought slightly below normal rainfall, late August precipitation helped to improve drought conditions across southern New Mexico in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: September started with Severe(D2) drought over the north and Moderate(D1) drought across the south.  By the end of the month, the entire zone was in Moderate(D1) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After several days of much drier conditions, a more traditional monsoon pattern returned as an area of high pressure developed near the Oklahoma and Texas border and a potent upper-level low pressure system developed off the coast of northern California. This resulted in southerly flow over New Mexico that allowed for moisture to stream back into the area. Scattered showers and thunderstorms made their return to western and central potions of the state. However, with so much dry air being in place previously, the atmosphere took time to moisten back up. Therefore, wetting rain wasn't common across much of the area. The exception was near Ruidoso on the McBride burn scar where an isolated thunderstorm resulted in flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Lincoln County emergency manager reported Gavilan Canyon Road near Janet Drive experienced flash flooding as heavy rain fell on the McBride burn scar. Damages are not known.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A break in the monsoon activity was finally in store for northern and central New Mexico, and this led to much quieter conditions through early September. However, a weak disturbance racing across the central Rockies plus a slight increase in mid-level moisture was enough to generate isolated thunderstorms across the northwest corner of the state on September 5, 2022. With drier low-levels, strong and gusty downburst winds were more common with these thunderstorms, and a 58 mph wind gust was briefly measured at the Farmington Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station in Farmington measured a brief wind gust of 58 mph due to strong downburst winds from nearby showers and thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The break in the monsoon activity in early September came to an end as Hurricane Kay went up the coast of Baja California and southern California. While Kay remained well off to the west of New Mexico, it was responsible for moisture advection into the area which led to an increase in thunderstorm activity on September 9, 2022. Strong thunderstorms developed over the northern mountains during the evening hours and propagated southward thanks to strong outflow boundaries. Winds associated with these outflow boundaries were severe as measured by weather stations in Stanley and Golden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds caused a 67 mph wind gust measured by a public weather station near Stanley.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Calf Canyon burn scar had received a well deserved break from flash flooding impacts for a couple of weeks at the end of August and into early September. However, monsoon season was not officially over yet, and a return of moisture resulted in numerous thunderstorms developing across the area on September 10, 2022. Unfortunately, the Calf Canyon burn scar was impacted as a nearly stationary thunderstorms dropped over two inches of rain on the southern portion of the large burn scar which caused flash flooding in the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A nearly stationary thunderstorm over El Porvenir produced over two inches of rainfall on this area of the Calf Canyon burn scar. This caused flash flooding on Beaver Creek and Gallinas Creek with significant debris found on Highway 65. The IMET on duty found several locations along Highway 65 that were covered in tree debris, rocks, and mud. Some buildings along the roadway were inundated with a couple of feet of water. The IMET also noted that water had threatened homes in the area as well, but these homes seemed to have been protected with sandbagging. Damages are estimated.

The pattern during the month of September favored the highest precipitation totals over western and northern New Mexico which saw the greatest drought improvement this time. Eastern New Mexico remained relatively drier, and as such, saw little to no change in drought conditions in this area. Precipitation totals across western and central New Mexico were generally around 1 to 2 inches during the month while totals less than one inch were more common across the east. About 31 percent of the state was in at least severe drought. About 7 percent of that was in extreme drought with no exceptional drought in the state since the beginning of 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from August 2022 continued in September.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The very active monsoon pattern over the past couple of months showed no signs of slowing during the month of September which contributed to the continued improvement in New Mexico drought conditions. The pattern during the month of September favored the highest precipitation totals over western and northern New Mexico which saw the greatest drought improvement this time. Eastern New Mexico remained relatively drier, and as such, saw little to no change in drought conditions in this area. Precipitation totals across western and central New Mexico were generally around 1 to 2 inches during the month while totals less than one inch were more common across the east. About 31 percent of the state was in at least severe drought. About 7 percent of that was in extreme drought with no exceptional drought in the state since the beginning of 2022.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions from August 2022 continued in September.

CZ_NAME: WEST SLOPES SACRAMENTO MOUNTAINS BELOW 7500 FEET
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though the last month of the monsoon season brought slightly below normal rainfall, late August precipitation helped to improve drought conditions across southern New Mexico in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone went from Severe(D2) drought to Moderate(D1) drought by the end of the month.  The Cooperative Observer in Mountain Park reported 2.25 inches of precipitation which was 0.23 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Even though the last month of the monsoon season brought slightly below normal rainfall, late August precipitation helped to improve drought conditions across southern New Mexico in September.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The zone went from Severe(D2) drought to Moderate(D1) drought by the end of the month.  The Cloudcroft Cooperative Observer reported 2.80 inches of precipitation which was 0.43 inches below normal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep fetch of subtropical moisture streaming over New Mexico allowed scattered to numerous showers and thunderstorms with heavy rainfall to impact parts of the region. A couple thunderstorms along the Continental Divide became strong with gusty outflow winds, frequent lightning strikes, and hail. One of the stronger storms produced a brief landspout tornado near Lake Valley, NM in San Juan County. No damage or injuries occurred with this brief storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A landspout tornado formed briefly about 10 miles north of Lake Valley, NM near State Road 371. No injuries or damage occurred with this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level storm system approaching from the Pacific northwest moved southeast through the Great Basin into the southern Rockies on November 24, 2022. A potent backdoor cold front also moved south through the Front Range and delivered strong winds to the eastern plains along with much colder air. The upper-level storm system then became nearly stationary over the Permian Basin through November 25, 2022 and allowed widespread rain and snow to impact areas from the central mountain chain into eastern NM. Wind gusts of 45 to 65 mph impacted eastern NM, especially the northeast plains where a semi-truck was blown over along U.S. Highway 64/87. Snowfall amounts ranged from four to eight inches primarily across the east central and southeast plains where a couple road closures occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several mesonet sites on the White Sands Missile Range reported peak wind gusts between 58 mph and 65 mph as northeast canyon winds developed behind a potent backdoor cold front.

CZ_NAME: JOHNSON AND BARTLETT MESAS INCLUDING RATON PASS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level storm system approaching from the Pacific northwest moved southeast through the Great Basin into the southern Rockies on November 24, 2022. A potent backdoor cold front also moved south through the Front Range and delivered strong winds to the eastern plains along with much colder air. The upper-level storm system then became nearly stationary over the Permian Basin through November 25, 2022 and allowed widespread rain and snow to impact areas from the central mountain chain into eastern NM. Wind gusts of 45 to 65 mph impacted eastern NM, especially the northeast plains where a semi-truck was blown over along U.S. Highway 64/87. Snowfall amounts ranged from four to eight inches primarily across the east central and southeast plains where a couple road closures occurred.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow and strong wind gusts up to 55 mph resulted in near-blizzard conditions along Interstate 25 at Raton Pass and portions of U.S. Highway 67/84 east of Raton. Five feet drifts were reported around Raton Pass. A semi-truck was blown over from high winds. Shockingly, no road closures were reported due to these impacts. Damage to the semi-truck is a rough estimate and there is no information on injuries.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper-level storm system moved quickly east across NM with widespread strong west to northwest winds on November 28-29, 2022. The strongest winds impacted areas along the central mountain chain into the nearby highlands where gusts of 55 to 75 mph were reported. A massive snarl of tumbleweeds collected at the Santa Fe airport. Some very light snow also fell over the Tusas Mountains with this system.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sandia Crest reported a peak wind gust up to 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As the remnants of what was once Hurricane Kay off the coast of southern California continued to bring beneficial tropical moisture into New Mexico on September 12, 2022, it led to another day of scattered showers and thunderstorms across the area. While heavy rainfall was observed with these thunderstorms along with slow storm motions, flash flooding reports were not received with the exception of the McBride burn scar. A brief thunderstorm developed over the burn area, and while rainfall totals were generally observed to be around or less than 0.10, the emergency manager reported that very localized heavy rainfall fell right over Gavilan Ridge. Unfortunately, this water then flowed directly onto Gavilan Canyon Road which has been significantly impacted this monsoon season.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Localized heavy rainfall on Gavilan Ridge flowed downhill onto the McBride burn scar which caused flash flooding along Gavilan Canyon Road from Janet Drive northward to Airplane Canyon. Burn scar flash flooding resulted in rock and mud debris along the roadway. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an abundant amount of tropical moisture still in place over New Mexico, it was another very active day across New Mexico as scattered thunderstorms developed on September 13, 2022. Steering flow was a lot faster compared to recent previous days which limited the flash flooding threat. However, thunderstorm activity near Roswell produced a strong downburst which resulted in brief severe winds being measured at the airport in the city.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Roswell briefly measured a 58 mph wind gust from strong downburst winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: November 2022 was much drier for most of the state with only a couple snowfall events across the northern high terrain. Very little improvement was made to drought conditions as most areas picked up less than one-quarter inch of precipitation for November. By months end, roughly 18 percent of the state was in severe to extreme drought, of which about 3 percent was extreme drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A small area of severe drought conditions from October 2022 persisted around the Four Corners through November.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mid-level trough extended northeastward from northern Mexico through the Permian Basin. Enhanced atmospheric lift and rich low and mid-level moisture were across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico. These conditions contributed to heavy rain. This rain moved over saturated soils and resulted in flash flooding across parts of West Texas and Southeast New Mexico.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain resulted in flash flooding in Eddy County. Several roads were closed along Black River near Malaga. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The break in the monsoon activity in early September came to an end as Hurricane Kay went up the coast of Baja California and southern California. While Kay remained well off to the west of New Mexico, it was responsible for moisture advection into the area which led to an increase in thunderstorm activity on September 9, 2022. Strong thunderstorms developed over the northern mountains during the evening hours and propagated southward thanks to strong outflow boundaries. Winds associated with these outflow boundaries were severe as measured by weather stations in Stanley and Golden.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm outflow winds caused a 65 mph wind gust at a public weather station near Golden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet passed over northern New Mexico and Colorado, producing repeated high wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph along and east of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on December 2, 2022. This included peak gusts in excess of 70 mph being reported at Las Vegas, Raton, and Clayton. Impacts from blowing dust reducing visibility also occurred, resulting in a multi-car accident being reported in Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Taos Ski Valley weather observing station recorded a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of forecast winter storms brought a mix of high winds, accumulating snow, and snow squalls to northern and central New Mexico. A strong section of the jetstream crossed areas along and east of the central mountain chain, starting over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains first before shifting south over the central highlands and south-central mountains. Peak wind gusts of 60 to 79 mph impacted these areas. Strong westerly winds also reached parts of the adjacent highlands and eastern plains, including Raton, Las Vegas, and Tucumcari. Winter impacts from snow focused mostly over western NM along the Continental Divide where the heaviest amounts of up to a foot were reported atop the Chuska Mountains, Jemez Mountains, and southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Snow squalls were also reported near Farmington and Santa Fe during the evening hours, producing near zero visibility and icy roads. Road closures also resulted from the snow packed and icy road conditions along I-40 from Grants to the AZ border, and along I-25 near La Bajada just south of Santa Fe near the Cochiti Pueblo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Angel Fire airport reported a wind gust of 79 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds associated with a strong section of the jetstream passed over northern NM. These strong winds impacted isolated and high elevation areas of the Jemez Mountains and Sangre de Cristo Mountains near Los Alamos and Taos Ski Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Los Alamos National Laboratories weather station recorded a peak gust of 61 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong northwest winds associated with a strong section of the jetstream passed over northern NM. These strong winds impacted isolated and high elevation areas of the Jemez Mountains and Sangre de Cristo Mountains near Los Alamos and Taos Ski Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Taos Ski Valley weather station atop Kachina Peak recorded a peak gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds impacted parts of the northeast highlands and high plains of central New Mexico. Peak gusts of 60 to 64 mph were felt at Las Vegas and near Vaughn starting in the morning hours and lasting into the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station southeast of Vaughn measured a wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong westerly winds impacted parts of the northeast highlands and high plains of central New Mexico. Peak gusts of 60 to 64 mph were felt at Las Vegas and near Vaughn starting in the morning hours and lasting into the early afternoon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Las Vegas Municipal Airport measured a wind gust of 63 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of forecast winter storms brought a mix of high winds, accumulating snow, and snow squalls to northern and central New Mexico. A strong section of the jetstream crossed areas along and east of the central mountain chain, starting over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains first before shifting south over the central highlands and south-central mountains. Peak wind gusts of 60 to 79 mph impacted these areas. Strong westerly winds also reached parts of the adjacent highlands and eastern plains, including Raton, Las Vegas, and Tucumcari. Winter impacts from snow focused mostly over western NM along the Continental Divide where the heaviest amounts of up to a foot were reported atop the Chuska Mountains, Jemez Mountains, and southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Snow squalls were also reported near Farmington and Santa Fe during the evening hours, producing near zero visibility and icy roads. Road closures also resulted from the snow packed and icy road conditions along I-40 from Grants to the AZ border, and along I-25 near La Bajada just south of Santa Fe near the Cochiti Pueblo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow squall. Visibility was estimated to have fallen to one quarter mile based on NMDOT webcams. A semi truck jackknifed on I-25 near La Bajada Hill later that morning due to remnant icy road conditions as a result of the snow squall. The extent of any injuries or damages is unknown.

Damages are a rough estimate for the roof being blown off.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper low was moving out of the Great Basin with a 120+ knot jet crossing southern New Mexico. In addition to the upper low, a lee side low developed over eastern Colorado and helped to bring wind gusts over 80 mph to parts of Otero County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 81 mph was reported at San Augustin Pass.  Other peak gusts across the zone included 79 mph at Salinas Peak, 76 mph at the White Sands Missile Range Emre site, 66 mph at Delta Clipper and 61 mph at the Pony site on the missile range.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A deep upper low was moving out of the Great Basin with an associated cold front. Moderate snow developed behind the cold front and brought over 6 inches to the higher elevations of the Gila Region.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The highest snow accumulation reported was 6.5 inches 11 miles northwest of Silver City.  Numerous other reports of 4 to 5.5 inches were reported within 15 miles of Silver City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Moisture values continued to be on the rise across northern and central New Mexico on July 20, 2022, and this led to a very active monsoon day for the area. Scattered to numerous thunderstorms were common across much of the area, particularly across the mountainous terrain. New Mexico's recent burn scars continued to  be magnets for heavy thunderstorms with known impacts across the McBride burn scar as well as the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar. Meanwhile, thunderstorms also produced brief strong winds in a couple locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain on the McBride burn scar caused flash flooding and debris flow within Gavilan Canyon. Photos shared by the Lincoln County emergency manager shows mud and debris across Gavilan Canyon Road which made the roadway impassable. Photos shared also depicted significant flooding and debris flow at the Gavilan Canyon Hideaway and Escape RV Park with mud and debris making roadways impassable. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Santa Fe, New Mexico area on June 25, 2022, caused minor flash flooding in the city with the arroyos filling up with water. Unfortunately, this resulted in the first arroyo flash flood fatality of the year after a man was found dead within an arroyo. However, there were other factors that contributed to his death.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain in the Santa Fe area caused the arroyos to fill up, and unfortunately, one man was found dead in the arroyo near Cerrillos and Rodeo. According to the Santa Fe emergency manager, the flooding within the arroyo was thought to be a contributing factor to the individual's death. However, the man also sustained blunt force trauma to the head and was also intoxicated, and these factors also contributed to his death. His age is unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A broad upper ridge was over the Gulf Coast states with Southeast New Mexico on the periphery of the upper ridge. Temperatures across the area were near or slightly above normal. Fairly high low-level moisture was across the area. These conditions resulted in a thunderstorm that produced wind damage in Eddy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced wind damage in Carlsbad. There were numerous reports of broken tree limbs in town. The cost of damage is a very rough estimate.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal rainfall in late June and July brought some relief to drought conditions across southern New Mexico in July.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire month started with the zone in Extreme(D3) drought, but by mid July, the zone was in Severe(D2) drought.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The entire zone started out in Severe(D2) drought but by the end of the month the northern half was in Moderate(D1) drought while the south remained in Severe(D2) drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level ridge was over Southeast New Mexico. Low-level moisture was fairly high across the area. These conditions resulted in a thunderstorm that produced a severe wind gust in Eddy County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm produced a 59 mph wind gust near Carlsbad at the ASOS site.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper high was located over New Mexico with some deep low and mid level moisture trapped under it over the Sacramento Mountains.  Training thunderstorms over the eastern slopes of the Sacramento Mountains brought up to 4 inches of rain to the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Otero County Emergency Manager reported up to 4 inches of rain had fallen in less than 2 hours at the Waterfall Lodge and along Cox Canyon Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some drier low level air started moving into southern New Mexico, but modest mid level moisture remained across the area. Gusty winds and flash flooding were reported with the stronger storms that developed in Grant and Otero counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter reported a peak wind gust of 59 mph two miles northeast of Gila.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Some drier low level air started moving into southern New Mexico, but modest mid level moisture remained across the area. Gusty winds and flash flooding were reported with the stronger storms that developed in Grant and Otero counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Landscaping and trash cans washed away in front of the Space Museum due to high water flows.  The radar estimated 1.5 to 2 inches of rainfall upstream of the museum over Horse Ridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture moved back into southern New Mexico from the southeast.  Light winds aloft kept storms moving slowly which allowed for heavy rain and flash flooding to occur in Grant and Otero counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dispatched reported Highway 35 was washed out from flooding out of Shingle Canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoon moisture moved back into southern New Mexico from the southeast.  Light winds aloft kept storms moving slowly which allowed for heavy rain and flash flooding to occur in Grant and Otero counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report of at least a foot of running water and debris over the road near the intersection of North Florida Ave and North Scenic Drive on the north side of Alamogordo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Modest low and mid level southeast flow kept some monsoon moisture around the Sacramento Mountains.  Very weak flow aloft kept storms moving slowly allowing for heavy rain to fall over the Soldier Canyon Burn Scar.  Several areas around Mescalero received flash flooding due to over two inches of rain falling in just over an hour.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A total of 2.25 inches of rain fell at the Newcalero Tribal Fish Hatchery in about an hour and a half.  Flash flooding was subsequently reported between mile markers 247 and 252 on Highway 70 with 3 feet of water and debris crossing the road. In addition to the highway flooding, six inches of water flowed into the Mescalero Community Center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Despite an early onset to the year's monsoon season and significant June rainfall totals, flash flooding and debris flow had not been reported across the largest burn scar in New Mexico likely thanks to steady, stratiform rain that had been occurring since mid-June. However, a more traditional monsoon pattern finally set up on July 1, 2022 as the high pressure to the east of New Mexico allowed for a deep plume of monsoonal moisture. Thunderstorms that developed over the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn area were now more convective and prone to producing heavy rainfall rates. This caused the most significant flash flooding so far of the season as multiple reports of flooding and debris flow across the burn scar were received. This prompted evacuations and road closures across western Mora and San Miguel counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 2 to 3 inches of rain across the northern portion of the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn area caused significant flash flooding and debris flow across portions of western Mora County. Canoncito Creek overtopped the culvert and flooded Canoncito Road. Highway 94 was also impacted with widespread flooding and debris flow which forced the closure of the highway. Damages are estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain near Lamy caused Old Lamy Trail Road to nearly wash out by the Legal Tender Saloon. Three to four feet of water was also reported along the railroad. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved through eastern and central New Mexico on June 1, 2022, ushered in low-level moisture and helped to set the stage for thunderstorm development during the afternoon of June 2nd. As the atmophere destabilized, thunderstorms developed along the high terrain as well as along the dryline. The highest severe weather threat for the day was across south central New Mexico where the nose of an upper-level jet provided the best wind shear. Hail and severe winds were the primary threats of the day, and a thunderstorm near Gran Quivira was reported to have quarter size hail. In southern Socorro County, wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported along thunderstorm outflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A White Sands Missile Range mesonet station south of Bingham measured a 66 mph wind gust likely associated with thunderstorm outflow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dryline sharpened across eastern New Mexico during the afternoon of June 3, 2022. As a storm system tracked across the central Rockies, its associated upper-level jet provided better wind shear across the area where there was also an abundant amount of instability. Thunderstorms developed early in the afternoon across the central high terrain, and then steering flow tracked these thunderstorms in an easterly direction onto the plains where they quickly intensified. Large hail was the primary concern with these severe thunderstorms. A thunderstorm in central San Miguel County likely produced the largest hail of the day with radar estimating two to three inch hail, however, its remote location provided no verification. In De Baca County, a long-duration and slow-moving complex of thunderstorms produced large hail near Yeso. In southwest Chaves County, a low precipitation supercell near Dunken that had numerous storm chasers on it had a well-defined wall cloud. While it produced hail up to one inch, a tornado never developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported quarter size hail from a severe thunderstorm along U.S. Highway 82 near Dunken.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After months of exceptionally dry, windy conditions that only worsened drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico, the month of June finally saw improvement thanks to an early onset of the monsoon season. Daily rounds of scattered to numerous thunderstorms brought welcome rainfall, especially across western and central New Mexico where the monsoonal moisture plume was focused most days. Two to six inches of rain across this area made the desert southwest some of the wettest areas in the continental United States during the month of June 2022, and these June rainfall totals were some of the highest in 50 years, about 5 to 10 standard deviations above normal. This finally resulted in drought improvements across the state with 95 percent of New Mexico in at least severe drought. About 58 percent of that was in at least extreme drought with about 12 percent in the exceptional category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Severe to extreme drought conditions from May 2022 improved to severe drought conditions across the area in June.

Thunderstorms developed early in the afternoon across the central high terrain, and then steering flow tracked these thunderstorms in an easterly direction onto the plains where they quickly intensified. Large hail was the primary concern with these severe thunderstorms. A thunderstorm in central San Miguel County likely produced the largest hail of the day with radar estimating two to three inch hail, however, its remote location provided no verification. In De Baca County, a long-duration and slow-moving complex of thunderstorms produced large hail near Yeso. In southwest Chaves County, a low precipitation supercell near Dunken that had numerous storm chasers on it had a well-defined wall cloud. While it produced hail up to one inch, a tornado never developed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A RAWS station near Mills reported a 59 mph thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a strong area of high pressure built over New Mexico on June 11, 2022, it led to soaring daytime temperatures. High temperatures during the afternoon reached well into the triple digits with some of the hottest temperatures experienced so far this year. Tucumcari reached a high temperature of 112 degrees Fahrenheit which actually set a new all time high temperature record which was previously 110 degrees Fahrenheit in 2020. Despite this high pressure system's presence, not all of the moisture was scoured out. Enough mid-level moisture was present to allow for isolated dry shower and thunderstorm activity. With very dry conditions in the low-levels, however, this activity produced very strong downburst winds. Las Vegas recorded a 59 mph wind gust with this dry activity.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Las Vegas measured a wind gust of 59 mph associated from gusty downburst winds from nearby shower and thunderstorm activity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After months of exceptionally dry, windy conditions that only worsened drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico, the month of June finally saw improvement thanks to an early onset of the monsoon season. Daily rounds of scattered to numerous thunderstorms brought welcome rainfall, especially across western and central New Mexico where the monsoonal moisture plume was focused most days. Two to six inches of rain across this area made the desert southwest some of the wettest areas in the continental United States during the month of June 2022, and these June rainfall totals were some of the highest in 50 years, about 5 to 10 standard deviations above normal. This finally resulted in drought improvements across the state with 95 percent of New Mexico in at least severe drought. About 58 percent of that was in at least extreme drought with about 12 percent in the exceptional category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from May 2022 improved to severe to extreme drought conditions across the area in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved through eastern New Mexico early on June 6, 2022, set the stage for a round of severe thunderstorms across northeast and east central New Mexico as it ushered it beneficial low-level moisture. With plentiful instability and wind shear across this area, the atmosphere quickly became supportive of strong to severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms developed first across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and then tracked in an easterly direction onto the plains where they strengthened. The strongest thunderstorm of the day started near Wagon Mound and then made a right turn before tracking southeastward through Harding, San Miguel, and Quay counties. When the thunderstorm was southwest of Roy, it developed a strong mesocyclone. While no mesocyclonic tornado was observed, a brief and weak landspout was. This thunderstorm's main hazard then was very large hail with hail up to baseball size reported near Bell Ranch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A spotter near Wagon Mound reported nickel size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With an area of high pressure still present over New Mexico on June 12, 2022, conditions at the surface still remained too dry for beneficial precipitation. Nontheless, mid-level moisture allowed for the development of high-based, dry thunderstorm activity across portions of central and eastern New Mexico during the afternoon and evening hours. This dry activity was prone to producing more gusty and erratic downburst winds, and several stations across Quay and De Baca counties measured wind gusts in excess of 60 mph.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Fort Sumner measured a 64 mph wind gust associated with a dry microburst. One inch diameter tree branches were downed with no other damage reported. Damages are estimated for the tree.

The 2022 monsoon season was officially underway, and with the moisture plume focused across the western half of the state, this was generally where thunderstorms developed throughout the afternoon and evening hours. While even the low-levels moistened up, dewpoint depressions were still large enough where the threat for strong downburst winds was a big concern. Shower and thunderstorm activity near Taos produced 60 to 75 mph wind gusts late in the evening. Meanwhile, fire weather season was not done with New Mexico yet as another wildfire was stated in eastern Bernalillo County along Interstate 40 and Carnuel Canyon that forced several evacuation as well as the closure of the Interstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An AWOS station near Taos measured a 58 mph wind gust associated with downburst winds from nearby shower activity.

CZ_NAME: EAST SLOPES SANGRE DE CRISTO MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon Fire, which were originally two separate wildfires that combined during the month of April 2022, continued to devastate the area during the month of June. By the end of June, this fire burned over 341,000 acres, still making it the largest wildfire in New Mexico State history.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: As the largest wildfire in New Mexico State history, the Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon Fire continued to devastate the area as it continued to grow. The good news for firefighting efforts was the early onset of the monsoon. Beneficial rainfall impacted the area as early as mid-June, helping firefighters and emergency personnel with containment. By the end of May, the wildfire had burned 315,627 acres with 50% containment. By the end of June, the wildfire had burned 341,735 acres with 93% containment. Full containment of this fire is anticipated in July.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cerro Pealdo Fire started in April 2022 and was finally contained by mid-June. The wildfire burned 45,605 acres in the Santa Fe National Forest.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cerro Pelado Fire, which began in April 2022 and grew rapidly during the month of May, was finally contained by June 15th. The wildfire burned 45,605 acres during this time. No known structures were burned during June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The Cerro Bandera fire began on private land within the El Malpais National Monument near Highway 53 in Cibola County. Dry and windy conditions allowed the fire to spread quickly. Four structures, including one home, were lost during the fire. The wildfire was contained on June 16th with 939 acres burned.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Cerro Bandera Fire began on June 9th on private land within the El Malpais National Monument. Dry and windy conditions allowed this fire to spread to 939 acres before it was contained on June 16th. Four structures, including one home, were destroyed. No other information was given. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was moving into Southeast New Mexico and West Texas. A dryline bisected the cold front and extended southward into the Big Bend. Plentiful low-level moisture was across the area ahead of the dryline. This moisture combined with temperatures in the mid-90s ahead of the cold front and resulted in an unstable atmosphere. Moderate wind shear was across the area. Lingering outflow boundaries from current and previous thunderstorms also aided in lift. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced hail and strong winds across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A mesonet station near Old Horse Springs measured a 64 mph wind gust from strong downburst winds from nearby shower activity.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of high pressure remained centered to the east of New Mexico, it allowed for a healthy stream of moisture to continue to move into the western half of the state on June 18, 2022. With the monsoonal moisture plume focused in this area, the day's thunderstorms favored areas along and west of the central mountain chain. With moisture streaming into the area over the past few days, the atmosphere was primed and better able to support thunderstorms with heavy rain. One of these thunderstorms near Embudo in Rio Arriba County produced an estimated 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain which caused flooding along area roadways. Another washed out a section of New Mexico State Road 6 near Los Lunas, forcing the closure of the highway.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An estimated 1.5 to 2.5 inches of rain caused an arroyo near Embudo to spill out of its bank, causing water to flow over County Road 69. Embudo Creek was also reported to be running high with clogged culverts at mile marker 3 on New Mexico State Road 75 near Dixon. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With the high pressure center to the east of New Mexico, a steady plume of moisture was present across western New Mexico on June 19, 2022. With these large moisture values, it was an active monsoon day across the area with scattered to numerous thunderstorms favored areas west of the central mountains. Cibola County was hit hardest as severe thunderstorms dropped large hail, and heavy rain caused flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter near San Fidel reported quarter size hail that had accumulated to about an inch deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: After months of exceptionally dry, windy conditions that only worsened drought conditions across northern and central New Mexico, the month of June finally saw improvement thanks to an early onset of the monsoon season. Daily rounds of scattered to numerous thunderstorms brought welcome rainfall, especially across western and central New Mexico where the monsoonal moisture plume was focused most days. Two to six inches of rain across this area made the desert southwest some of the wettest areas in the continental United States during the month of June 2022, and these June rainfall totals were some of the highest in 50 years, about 5 to 10 standard deviations above normal. This finally resulted in drought improvements across the state with 95 percent of New Mexico in at least severe drought. About 58 percent of that was in at least extreme drought with about 12 percent in the exceptional category.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extreme drought conditions from May 2022 improved to severe drought conditions in June.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A persistent pattern continued on June 30, 2022, as an area of high pressure to the east of New Mexico allowed for a deep moisture plume across the area. With this moisture plume focused across western New Mexico, the afternoon and evening thunderstorms favored areas along and west of the central mountain chain. The Hermits Peak and Calf Canyon burn scar, which for several days had received steady, stratiform rain, finally responded as heavy rain resulted flash flooding and debris flow on the northern end of the large burn scar in Mora County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mora County emergency manager reported that the Canoncito Creek overtopped the culvert and flooded Canoncito Road. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually deep moisture for late June was located over southwest New Mexico with some slow moving storms dropping significant rains across the area which led to flash flooding around Mesquite and Hillsboro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mud and debris were across Highway 28 at mile marker 22.  Hillsboro during the overnight hours received around 1.50 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unusually deep moisture for late June was located over southwest New Mexico with some slow moving storms dropping significant rains across the area which led to flash flooding around Mesquite and Hillsboro.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A CoCoRaHS observer received 4.30 inches of rain.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep monsoonal moisture remained across the region just ahead of an upper trough moving across the southern Rockies.  Storms developed off the eastern slopes of the Sacramento Mountains with some isolated areas receiving heavy rain which caused flash flooding along Highway 130.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Highway 130 was closed due to widespread flash flooding west of Weed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front was moving into Southeast New Mexico and West Texas. A dryline bisected the cold front and extended southward into the Big Bend. Plentiful low-level moisture was across the area ahead of the dryline. This moisture combined with temperatures in the mid-90s ahead of the cold front and resulted in an unstable atmosphere. Moderate wind shear was across the area. Lingering outflow boundaries from current and previous thunderstorms also aided in lift. These conditions resulted in thunderstorms that produced hail and strong winds across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico.

As an area of high pressure centered itself to the east of New Mexico, this aided in the development of a monsoon moisture plume across Arizona and western New Mexico on June 16, 2022. The 2022 monsoon season was officially underway, and with the moisture plume focused across the western half of the state, this was generally where thunderstorms developed throughout the afternoon and evening hours. While even the low-levels moistened up, dewpoint depressions were still large enough where the threat for strong downburst winds was a big concern. Shower and thunderstorm activity near Taos produced 60 to 75 mph wind gusts late in the evening. Meanwhile, fire weather season was not done with New Mexico yet as another wildfire was stated in eastern Bernalillo County along Interstate 40 and Carnuel Canyon that forced several evacuation as well as the closure of the Interstate.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Sixty-Six Fire was first reported on June 16th off of Interstate 40 in Carnuel. Dry and windy conditions allowed the fire to spread to 26 acres before it was fully contained on June 21st. Unfortnately, one home, two outbuildings, and one power pole were burned or significantly damaged. No other information was provided. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The monsoonal moisture plume continued to be focused across the western half of New Mexico on June 17, 2022. This led to another active day across this area as thunderstorms developed across the high terrain in the early afternoon before becoming more widespread across lower elevations as the day progressed. Steering flow moved this activity in a south to north direction. With improving moisture advection, heavy rainfall was more common with the strongest thunderstorms. While this prompted Flash Flood Warnings for the Cerro Pelado burn scar, no impacts were known. Meanwhile, Albuquerque broke a 78 day streak with no measurable precipitation when thunderstorms finally impacted the metro area. Farther north in Farmington, strong downburst winds were responsible for a 60 mph gust.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station near Farmington measured a 60 mph wind gust associated from strong downburst winds from nearby showers.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As an area of high pressure remained centered east of New Mexico, deep monsoonal moisture continued to surge into northern and central New Mexico on June 26, 2022. An embedded easterly wave helped in the development and intensification of numerous thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening hours. Northeast Roswell was hit hardest as thunderstorms with heavy rainfall rates caused flash flooding across portions of the city. A blocked drainage gate was responsible for flooding at least a dozen homes in the Holly Loop subdivision, and elsewhere in Roswell, an overflowing Borrendo Creek caused a bridge to collapse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Over three inches of rain in Roswell caused significant flooding on the northeast side of the city. At least twelve homes in the Holly Loop subdividion were flooded, and emergency crews had to divert water through cinder block walls away from the subdivision and onto East Country Club Road. Farther east, a small bridge collapsed at East Mescalero Road near La Jara Road due to flood waters in Borrendo Creek. Damages are estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The dryline brought deeper moisture back into areas east of the Rio Grande Valley.  A deep upper low was located over the Baja peninsula and an upper level disturbance lifting north out of it helped to trigger several severe thunderstorms over South Central New Mexico producing hail up to the size of quarters and 74 mph wind gusts.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public in Crownpoint spotted a funnel cloud that did not make contact with the ground.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another backdoor cold front surged through eastern and central New Mexico early in the day on June 8, 2022, helping to reinforce the moisture that had been present all week across the plains and pushing it into the Rio Grande Valley. This backdoor cold front was responsible for the development of low stratus along the east slopes of the central mountain chain and areas eastward which generally prevented the development of thunderstorms across the plains. However, the increase in moisture along with an unstable atmosphere across the Rio Grande Valley did allow for thunderstorm development there. Weak wind shear though prevented long lasting severe thunderstorms. As such, any thunderstorms that did manage to become strong were generally short-lived. One report of quarter size hail was reported near San Ysidro while another large hail report was received near Cimarron Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A member of the public near San Ysidro reported quarter size hail.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak upper ridge was over West Texas and Southeast New Mexico, and upper disturbances were moving overhead. These upper disturbances, along with remnant outflow boundaries from previous thunderstorms, helped to provide lift. Low-level moisture, instability, and wind shear were high across the area. These conditions resulted in a thunderstorm that produced flash flooding in the Western Low Rolling Plains during the early morning hours. Thunderstorms then redeveloped and produced hail and strong winds across West Texas and Southeast New Mexico during the evening hours.

CZ_NAME: MIDDLE RIO GRANDE VALLEY/ALBUQUERQUE METRO AREA
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front moved into eastern New Mexico on May 31, 2022, and was then reinforced through central New Mexico on June 1st. As this backdoor cold front pushed through the gaps of the central mountain chain late on June 1st, it produced a strong east canyon wind in Tijeras Canyon and the east side of the Albuquerque metro area. A 59 mph wind gust was measured at the Albuquerque International Airport.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ASOS station at the Albuquerque International Airport measured a peak wind gust of 59 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved through eastern and central New Mexico on June 1, 2022, ushered in low-level moisture and helped to set the stage for thunderstorm development during the afternoon of June 2nd. As the atmophere destabilized, thunderstorms developed along the high terrain as well as along the dryline. The highest severe weather threat for the day was across south central New Mexico where the nose of an upper-level jet provided the best wind shear. Hail and severe winds were the primary threats of the day, and a thunderstorm near Gran Quivira was reported to have quarter size hail. In southern Socorro County, wind gusts in excess of 60 mph were reported along thunderstorm outflow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A COOP observer near Gran Quivira reported quarter size hail with a severe thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A backdoor cold front that moved through eastern New Mexico early on June 6, 2022, set the stage for a round of severe thunderstorms across northeast and east central New Mexico as it ushered it beneficial low-level moisture. With plentiful instability and wind shear across this area, the atmosphere quickly became supportive of strong to severe thunderstorms. Thunderstorms developed first across the Sangre de Cristo Mountains and then tracked in an easterly direction onto the plains where they strengthened. The strongest thunderstorm of the day started near Wagon Mound and then made a right turn before tracking southeastward through Harding, San Miguel, and Quay counties. When the thunderstorm was southwest of Roy, it developed a strong mesocyclone. While no mesocyclonic tornado was observed, a brief and weak landspout was. This thunderstorm's main hazard then was very large hail with hail up to baseball size reported near Bell Ranch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser reported a brief, weak landspout tornado over open rangeland near Maes. No damage was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous days of heavy rain raised levels of the Gila River to moderate or greater flooding levels.  A creek that feeds the Gila River also experienced flooding and caused a bridge on Highway 180 to collapse.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A bridge collapsed on US 180 between Silver City and Cliff due to flooding on a creek that feeds the Gila River. It took several days to repair the bridge. In addition one structure near the river was washed off it's foundation.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture remained over the region with an upper trough approaching from the west.  Weak mid-level flow kept storms slow moving resulting in areas receiving over 2 inches of rain leading to flash flooding and debris flows around the Soldier Canyon burn area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Recent heavy rain and an additional 1 to 3 inches of rain brought debris flow down from the Soldier Canyon burn scar.  The Emergency Manager reported Round Mountain Road was closed from 4 feet of mud and debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong upper level jet passed over northern New Mexico and Colorado, producing repeated high wind gusts of 60 to 75 mph along and east of the Sangre de Cristo Mountains on December 2, 2022. This included peak gusts in excess of 70 mph being reported at Las Vegas, Raton, and Clayton. Impacts from blowing dust reducing visibility also occurred, resulting in a multi-car accident being reported in Clayton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public weather station near Santa Rosa Lake State Park experienced a peak wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A potent Arctic airmass surged through eastern NM the night of December 21st through December 22nd bringing near zero to sub-zero temperatures and strong winds. The combination of these produced wind chills that fell to the negative teens, twenties, and thirties through east-central and northeastern NM.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Clayton Airport reported a wind chill of -33F.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The first in a series of forecast winter storms brought a mix of high winds, accumulating snow, and snow squalls to northern and central New Mexico. A strong section of the jetstream crossed areas along and east of the central mountain chain, starting over the Sangre de Cristo Mountains first before shifting south over the central highlands and south-central mountains. Peak wind gusts of 60 to 79 mph impacted these areas. Strong westerly winds also reached parts of the adjacent highlands and eastern plains, including Raton, Las Vegas, and Tucumcari. Winter impacts from snow focused mostly over western NM along the Continental Divide where the heaviest amounts of up to a foot were reported atop the Chuska Mountains, Jemez Mountains, and southern Sangre de Cristo Mountains. Snow squalls were also reported near Farmington and Santa Fe during the evening hours, producing near zero visibility and icy roads. Road closures also resulted from the snow packed and icy road conditions along I-40 from Grants to the AZ border, and along I-25 near La Bajada just south of Santa Fe near the Cochiti Pueblo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow impacted the Chuska Mountains. A SNOTEL site reported 14 inches of snow.

Snow squalls were also reported near Farmington and Santa Fe during the evening hours, producing near zero visibility and icy roads. Road closures also resulted from the snow packed and icy road conditions along I-40 from Grants to the AZ border, and along I-25 near La Bajada just south of Santa Fe near the Cochiti Pueblo.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Taos Ski Valley reported a wind gust of 64 mph at Kachina Peak.
